title: INTRODUCTION TO AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of the aerospace engineering discipline. It explores the fundamental principles governing flight, the design and operation of aircraft and spacecraft, and the key systems involved. Students will be introduced to the history, scope, and future directions of aerospace technology, along with essential engineering practices and safety considerations within the industry.

title: MECHANICS OF AEROSPACE STRUCTUES
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the design, analysis, and integrity of aerospace structures. It covers the behavior of aircraft components under various operational loads, including static and dynamic forces, and examines material selection and structural configurations. Emphasis is placed on applying engineering analysis techniques to ensure structural safety, performance, and compliance with airworthiness standards throughout the aircraft lifecycle.

title: ORBITAL MECHANICS I
description: AAE 335 provides a comprehensive study of orbital mechanics, covering fundamental dynamics and practical applications for engineering students. The course delves into the mathematics of spacecraft trajectories, orbital maneuvers, and interplanetary travel. It builds a strong foundation for analyzing and designing space missions.

outline.title: Orbital Maneuvers: Hohmann Transfers
subtopics: Non-Hohmann transfers with a common apse line

title: ORBITAL MECHANICS II
description: AAE 336 explores the fundamental principles of orbital mechanics, essential for aerospace engineering students. The course covers orbital position determination, utilizing perifocal coordinate systems and mathematical techniques for analyzing orbital trajectories. Emphasis is placed on both theoretical understanding and practical application, including computational methods using MATLAB.

outline.title: Keplerian Elements
subtopics: Orbital Position as a Function of Time

outline.title: Orbital Position as a Function of Time

title: ANALYSIS OF AEROSPACE STRUCTURAL MECHANICS I
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of applied mechanics principles essential for analyzing aerospace structures. It covers the behavior of bodies at rest and in motion, exploring concepts of statics, dynamics, kinematics, and kinetics. Emphasis is placed on understanding forces, motion, material properties including anisotropy relevant to composites, and energy methods for structural analysis.

title: AERODYNAMICS I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the behavior of air and other fluids in motion, focusing on the aerodynamic forces and phenomena experienced by aircraft. It delves into the characteristics of incompressible and compressible flows, including concepts like Mach number and isentropic processes. Students will analyze airfoil behavior, understand the generation of lift and drag, and examine how atmospheric conditions affect flight performance. The curriculum also covers essential aspects of wind tunnel testing and the thermodynamic principles underlying fluid dynamics.

title: AERODYNAMICS II
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing high-speed fluid flow, with a focus on compressible gases. It delves into the analysis of isentropic flow through nozzles and the behavior of shock waves, including thermodynamic property changes. Students will learn to apply these concepts to practical problems in wind tunnel design and operation, utilizing various unit systems and aerodynamic data sources.

title: THERMODYNAMICS FOR AEROSPACE ENGINEERING
description: Thermodynamics is a fundamental science for engineering, focusing on energy transformations, heat, and work. This course applies these principles to aerospace systems like propulsion, power generation, and refrigeration. It covers fundamental laws, thermodynamic cycles, and their application in analyzing system performance.

title: AIRCRAFT MATERIALS
description: This course explores the fundamental properties and behavior of materials used in aircraft structures. It covers material selection, performance under various loads and environmental conditions, and the mechanisms of material failure, particularly fatigue. Emphasis is placed on design criteria, analysis techniques, and non-destructive testing methods essential for ensuring structural integrity and safety throughout an aircraft's service life.

title: AIRCRAFT FLIGHT PRINCIPLES I
description: AAE 347 provides a foundational understanding of the physical principles governing aircraft flight. This course explores aerodynamics, stability, and performance, drawing from both fundamental aerodynamic theory and practical applications in aircraft design. It is intended as introductory material for students in aeronautical engineering and those with a general interest in aviation.

title: AIRCRAFT FLIGHT PRINCIPLES II
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing the operation and application of aircraft instrumentation. It explores the physics and engineering behind key flight instruments, including those measuring airspeed, altitude, attitude, and heading. Emphasis is placed on how these systems provide critical information for safe flight, navigation, and aircraft control, as well as their integration into modern avionics. The academic scope covers both traditional electromechanical instruments and contemporary digital display systems.

title: AIRCRAFT DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the motion and stability of aircraft. It covers the mathematical models used to describe aircraft behavior, including the derivation of equations of motion and the analysis of aerodynamic forces. Students will investigate concepts of static and dynamic stability, understand coordinate systems, and analyze the impact of control surfaces on aircraft response.

title: AEROSPACE TEHCNOLOGY LABORATORY
description: AAE 361 is a 300-level propulsion laboratory course offered by the Department of Aeronautical and Astronautical Engineering at Afe Babalola University. This course provides hands-on experience with experimental procedures and emphasizes meticulous record-keeping, including showing all working before inputting values. Students will learn to write comprehensive laboratory reports and adhere to strict safety protocols within the laboratory environment.

title: AEROSPACE TECHNOLOGY LAB II
description: This laboratory course provides hands-on experience in applying fundamental aerospace engineering principles. Students will utilize computational tools for design and analysis, explore advanced manufacturing techniques, and investigate the performance characteristics of aircraft systems. The curriculum emphasizes practical problem-solving related to aerodynamics, thermodynamics, propulsion, and flight mechanics.

title: AEROSPACE NUMERICAL METHODS
description: This course explores numerical techniques for solving differential equations relevant to aerospace engineering. It focuses on the Finite Difference Method (FDM) for discretizing continuous problems into algebraic systems. Topics include derivative approximations, solving boundary value problems, and applying these methods to both ordinary and partial differential equations, including time-dependent phenomena.

title: CONTROL SYSTEMS OF AAE
description: This course explores the principles and applications of advanced control systems tailored for aeronautical and astronautical engineering. It covers the mathematical modeling of complex aerospace vehicles and their subsystems, focusing on the analysis of stability and performance. Students will learn to design and implement controllers for flight control, propulsion management, and guidance, considering real-world environmental factors and system dynamics.

title: AIRCRAFT AND SPACECRAFT PROPULSION
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing the generation of thrust for aircraft and spacecraft. It explores various engine cycles, performance analysis, and the critical impact of environmental factors such as altitude and speed. Students will analyze different engine types, calculate key performance metrics like thrust and efficiency, and understand the design considerations for propulsion systems in aerospace applications.

title: COMBUSTION PROCESSES
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the release of energy through chemical reactions, with a focus on aerospace applications. It delves into the thermodynamics and kinetics of combustion processes, examining factors that influence reaction rates and energy output. The curriculum also covers the practical aspects of combustion within propulsion systems, including heat transfer phenomena and the behavior of flames under various conditions, as well as the impact of atmospheric conditions on performance.

title: ANALYSISNOF AEROSPACE STRUCTURAL MECHANICS II
description: This course delves into the advanced analysis of aerospace structures, focusing on their behavior under various operational loads and environmental conditions. It covers critical aspects of structural integrity, fatigue life prediction, material behavior, and failure mechanisms. The academic scope includes understanding how to ensure the safety, reliability, and durability of aircraft structures throughout their service life.

title: INTRODUCTION TO TURBULENT FLOWS
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing turbulent fluid flows, a critical phenomenon in aerospace engineering. It explores the mathematical descriptions of turbulence, including the Navier-Stokes equations and their statistical treatments. The curriculum covers the physical mechanisms of turbulence generation, energy dissipation, and the dynamics of turbulent boundary layers. Students will also learn about numerical simulation techniques and the importance of transport phenomena in aerodynamic analysis.

title: DYNAMICS AND CONTROL OF SPACECRAFTS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the motion and orientation of aerospace vehicles. It delves into the mathematical modeling of dynamic systems, including the derivation of equations of motion and the analysis of stability characteristics. Emphasis is placed on understanding how external forces and internal control mechanisms influence vehicle behavior. The academic scope covers the theoretical underpinnings necessary for designing and analyzing aerospace vehicle performance and control.

title: AIRCRAFT PERFORMANCE
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing how aircraft operate and achieve flight. It explores the interplay between aerodynamic forces, engine thrust, and aircraft weight to determine flight capabilities. Key areas include analyzing aircraft speeds, takeoff and landing performance, fuel efficiency, and the impact of atmospheric conditions on flight envelopes. Students will learn to calculate and predict aircraft performance under various operational scenarios.

title: AEROSPACE SYSTEMS DESIGN
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles and advanced methodologies for designing aerospace vehicles. It covers the analysis of aerodynamic configurations, propulsion systems, and overall aircraft performance. Students will explore the integration of various aircraft systems, the application of modern design and manufacturing tools, and the critical aspects of airworthiness and regulatory compliance throughout the aerospace product lifecycle.

title: AIRCRAFT MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of aircraft maintenance, focusing on reliability, maintainability, and airworthiness. It examines various maintenance strategies, regulatory frameworks, and the management functions essential for ensuring operational safety and efficiency. Students will gain an understanding of key metrics, processes, and organizational structures within the aviation maintenance, repair, and overhaul (MRO) sector.

title: HEAT TRANSFER
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the transfer of thermal energy. It delves into the mechanisms of conduction, convection, and radiation, and their interplay in various engineering systems. Emphasis is placed on developing analytical skills to predict thermal behavior, understand energy transport phenomena, and solve complex heat transfer problems encountered in aerospace applications.

title: SYSTEMS MODELLING AND SIMULATION
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced techniques for mathematical modeling and simulation of complex systems. It covers numerical methods for solving differential equations and statistical analysis, alongside their application in aerospace engineering. Students will learn to model physical phenomena, analyze system performance, and evaluate design concepts using computational tools and data. The academic scope includes developing and applying models for aircraft performance, atmospheric conditions, and space system development.

title: COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
description: This course delves into the numerical simulation of fluid flow phenomena. It covers the fundamental governing equations of fluid dynamics, including the Navier-Stokes equations, and explores various numerical methods for their discretization and solution. Students will learn the principles of setting up, executing, and interpreting CFD simulations, with a focus on turbulence modeling and applications in aerospace engineering.

title: AIRCRAFT STABILITY AND CONTROL
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing the dynamic behavior of aircraft. It explores the concepts of static and dynamic stability, analyzing how aircraft respond to disturbances and return to equilibrium. Emphasis is placed on understanding the aerodynamic forces and moments that influence stability, as well as the control surfaces and systems used to maintain desired flight paths.

title: AIRCRAFT CONCEPTUAL DESIGN II
description: This course focuses on the initial stages of aircraft design, emphasizing the translation of requirements into feasible aircraft concepts. It covers the integration of critical factors such as airworthiness regulations, performance estimation, and load considerations into early design decisions. Students will explore the application of modern design tools and methodologies, understand the impact of industry standards, and learn to define preliminary aircraft configurations and system architectures. The academic scope involves developing a foundational understanding of how to conceptualize and evaluate aircraft designs based on performance, safety, and operational constraints.

title: GAS TURBINE PROPULSION
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the operation and performance of gas turbine engines for aerospace applications. It covers the thermodynamic cycles, component functionalities, and various engine types, including turbojets, turbofans, and turboprops. Emphasis is placed on analyzing thrust generation, fuel efficiency, and the impact of flight conditions on engine performance, integrating propulsion concepts with aircraft flight mechanics.

title: AIRCRAFT CONCEPTUAL DESIGN I
description: This course explores the initial stages of aircraft development, transforming high-level requirements into feasible design concepts. It covers the fundamental principles of aerodynamics, structures, propulsion, and systems integration. Students will learn to evaluate design trade-offs and ensure compliance with airworthiness standards during the early design phases.

title: RELIABILITY AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles of ensuring aircraft safety, structural integrity, and system functionality throughout their lifecycle. It covers the rigorous processes involved in monitoring aircraft performance, analyzing failures, and implementing corrective actions. The academic scope includes understanding regulatory frameworks, managing design and production quality, approving maintenance operations, and maintaining essential aircraft data for continued airworthiness.

title: SPACE SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practices of developing complex space systems. It covers the entire lifecycle, from initial concept and design to project management, scheduling, and reliability analysis. Students will learn about the phases of space system development and methods for effective resource management.

title: SPACECRAFT FLIGHT
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the design, analysis, and operation of spacecraft. It delves into the complexities of space environments, propulsion systems, and orbital mechanics essential for successful space missions. Students will gain an understanding of spacecraft systems integration, launch and re-entry dynamics, and operational safety protocols.

title: SPACE VEHICLE DEVELOPMENT
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and processes involved in the development of vehicles designed for space missions. It covers the unique environmental challenges of space, the design considerations for critical subsystems, and the methodologies for ensuring structural integrity and operational success. Emphasis is placed on project management techniques, payload integration, the use of design and manufacturing tools, and the rigorous testing required for space-qualified hardware.

title: AVIONICS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the intricate world of aircraft electronic and software systems, focusing on their design, integration, and operational integrity. It emphasizes the principles of airworthiness, system performance monitoring, and the critical role of safety in aviation operations. Students will gain insights into certification processes, maintenance strategies, and the management of system failures to ensure the reliability and safety of aircraft throughout their service life.

title: AVIATION SAFETY AND LAW
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing aviation safety and the legal frameworks that ensure its integrity. It delves into the roles and responsibilities of regulatory bodies, the establishment and maintenance of airworthiness standards, and the critical processes of aircraft certification and continued oversight. Emphasis is placed on understanding safety management systems, accident investigation, and the integration of quality management to uphold operational excellence and public trust in the aviation industry.

title: AIRPORT DESIGN AND MANAGEMENT
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical considerations involved in designing and managing airport facilities. It covers the strategic placement of airports, the intricate planning of airside operations including runways and taxiways, and the layout of passenger and cargo handling areas. Emphasis is placed on understanding design standards, capacity requirements, and common challenges to ensure efficient, safe, and sustainable airport development.

title: RESEARCH PROJECT
description: This course offers students the opportunity to conduct an in-depth, independent investigation within a specialized area of aeronautical or astronautical engineering. It emphasizes the development of critical research skills, including problem formulation, comprehensive literature review, rigorous analytical or experimental design, and the interpretation of complex data. The academic scope involves applying advanced engineering principles to address challenging technical issues, culminating in a detailed project report and presentation.

title: HEALTH COMMUNICATION
description: This course explores fundamental principles of health and wellness, covering various dimensions of well-being and factors influencing it. It delves into common health issues, disease prevention, and responsible decision-making regarding substance use and sexual health. Emphasis is placed on developing effective communication skills, understanding interpersonal dynamics, and promoting health awareness within oneself and potentially in group settings.

title: LEADERSHIP STUDIES
description: This course explores the fundamental concept of leadership, examining its various scholarly definitions and historical context. It delves into the essential principles and qualities that define effective leadership, alongside different leadership styles and types. Students will also be introduced to foundational theories and sources of leadership power.

title: FRENCH LANGUAGE I
description: This introductory course focuses on developing foundational communication skills in a foreign language. Students will learn essential vocabulary, grammatical structures, and pronunciation necessary for basic interactions. The academic scope includes practical application of linguistic elements in everyday contexts.

title: IGBO LANGUAGE I
description: This foundational course introduces students to the core elements of a new language, focusing on developing essential communication skills. Learners will acquire fundamental vocabulary, grasp basic grammatical structures, and practice simple conversational exchanges. The course also explores the cultural context intertwined with the language, aiming to foster effective cross-cultural interaction and linguistic competence.

title: FOOD PRODUCTION
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practices involved in transforming raw agricultural materials into consumable food products. It covers the entire production chain, from sourcing raw ingredients to processing, preservation, and ensuring final product quality and safety. Students will examine various technologies, system designs, and operational considerations, including automation and safety protocols, essential for efficient and reliable food manufacturing. The academic scope encompasses both the scientific basis of food transformation and the engineering aspects of production systems.

title: FRENCH LANGUAGE II
description: This course focuses on developing intermediate-level proficiency in the French language. It aims to enhance students' ability to understand, speak, read, and write French through the study of grammar, vocabulary, and communicative functions. Emphasis is placed on practical application and building confidence in using the language in various contexts.

title: IGBO LANGUAGE II
description: This course explores the rich cultural heritage of the Igbo people, focusing on significant traditional practices and their societal roles. Students will delve into the intricacies of cultural expressions, examining their historical context and contemporary relevance. The academic scope encompasses linguistic elements embedded within cultural narratives and performances.

outline.title: Introduction to Igbo Culture and Mmanwu

title: PRACTICAL ENTREPRENEURSHIP
description: This course explores the foundational elements of establishing and managing a water production enterprise. It delves into critical aspects such as market analysis, facility planning, and operational setup. Students will gain insights into the technical processes of water treatment, manufacturing, quality control, and regulatory compliance necessary for a successful venture.

title: HUMAN ANATOMY I
description: This course introduces the fundamental principles of human anatomy, focusing on the structural organization of the body from microscopic to macroscopic levels. It covers the basic components of cells, tissues, and the major organ systems, emphasizing their relationships and functions. The academic scope includes detailed study of skeletal, muscular, nervous, cardiovascular, respiratory, and digestive systems.

title: HUMAN ANATOMY II
description: This course delves into the intricate structures of the human body, building upon foundational anatomical knowledge. It explores the complex systems responsible for physiological functions, examining their macroscopic and microscopic organization. Emphasis is placed on understanding the relationships between structure and function across various organ systems.

title: GENERAL BIOLOGY I
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of biological principles, exploring the fundamental units of life, cellular processes, and the mechanisms of inheritance. It delves into the organization of living organisms from tissues to reproductive strategies. The academic scope covers cell structure and function, cell division, genetic concepts, and the diversity of biological tissues and their roles.

title: GENERAL BIOLOGY II
description: This course explores fundamental biological processes at the cellular and tissue levels. It delves into the intricate mechanisms governing cell division and regulation, as well as the diverse structures and functions of various tissue types within organisms. The academic scope covers essential concepts for understanding biological systems and their organization.

title: GENERAL PRACTICAL BIOLOGY I
description: This introductory course explores fundamental biological concepts, including the mechanics and regulation of the cell cycle and the structure and function of biological tissues. It also covers essential principles of health, wellness, and disease prevention, providing a broad overview of human biology and well-being. The academic scope bridges basic life sciences with an awareness of health factors crucial for science students.

title: GENERAL PRACTICAL BIOLOGY
description: This course explores fundamental biological processes at the cellular and tissue levels. It delves into the mechanics of cell division and its regulation, alongside the diverse structures and functions of biological tissues. Additionally, the course examines foundational concepts of health, disease mechanisms, and the physiological underpinnings of wellness. The academic scope covers core principles essential for understanding living organisms.

title: INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of the interdisciplinary field that integrates engineering principles with biological and medical sciences. It explores the design, development, and application of devices, materials, and systems to solve problems in biology and medicine. Students will be introduced to key areas such as biomaterials, biomechanics, instrumentation, and the fundamental sciences underpinning biomedical applications.

title: MEASUREMENT AND INSTRUMENTATION
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles and practical applications of measurement and instrumentation within the biomedical engineering context. It covers the characteristics of various measuring instruments, techniques for analyzing and minimizing errors, and the dynamic behavior of measurement systems. Students will explore the design and application of common instruments used for electrical and physical quantity measurement.

title: BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS CONTROL AND MODELLING
description: This course provides a foundation in the mathematical modelling of biological systems, drawing from principles of fluid mechanics and thermodynamics. It examines the behavior of biological fluids, including their flow dynamics, rheological properties, and transport in physiological systems. Additionally, the course explores thermodynamic laws governing spontaneity and equilibrium, enabling the analysis of energy transformations and chemical processes within living organisms.

title: BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING I
description: This course introduces the fundamental principles of electrical measurement and instrumentation as applied to biomedical engineering. It delves into the theory behind measuring electrical quantities, the design and function of amplifier circuits for biological signals, and the architecture of instrumentation systems. Emphasis is placed on understanding signal acquisition, processing, and the practical considerations for designing reliable biomedical measurement devices.

title: BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING II
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles and applications of electronic systems within biomedical engineering. It explores the design, operation, and selection of sensors, instrumentation, and devices used for medical diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment. Emphasis is placed on understanding material properties, biocompatibility, fluid dynamics, and design methodologies relevant to creating safe and effective biomedical technologies. The academic scope includes principles of measurement, device reliability, and the thermodynamic considerations of biological systems.

title: BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING LAB I
description: This course offers hands-on experience with fundamental principles in biomedical engineering. Students will engage in practical investigations of biomaterial properties, biological fluid mechanics, and essential measurement techniques. The curriculum covers material characterization, fluid rheology, instrumentation for biological systems, and key concepts from biochemistry and thermodynamics relevant to engineering applications.

title: BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING LAB II
description: This laboratory course provides hands-on experience in characterizing and evaluating materials used in biomedical applications. Students will explore the fundamental principles of biomaterial science, including their properties, selection criteria, and evaluation methods. The curriculum also covers the mechanics of biological fluids, rheological behaviors, and essential measurement and instrumentation techniques relevant to biomedical engineering. Emphasis is placed on understanding material behavior, fluid dynamics, and the application of analytical tools in a laboratory setting.

title: MECHANICS OF BIOLOGICAL FLUIDS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the behavior of biological fluids. It delves into the continuum hypothesis, fluid kinematics, and statics, examining pressure distributions and motion characteristics. The curriculum also covers rheological properties of fluids like blood, transport phenomena, and the application of fluid mechanics principles to physiological systems.

title: SYSTEMS BIOENGINEERING I
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of engineering principles applied to biological systems. It integrates concepts from thermodynamics, biomaterials science, and fluid mechanics to analyze biological functions and structures. The academic scope covers the quantitative modeling of biological processes, material interactions within the body, and the dynamics of biological fluids, preparing students for advanced bioengineering system design and analysis.

title: THEORY OF DESIGN FOR BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
description: This course delves into the fundamental theories and principles governing the design of biomedical devices. It explores the critical aspects of material selection, evaluation, and mechanical analysis, ensuring devices meet stringent safety and performance standards. Students will learn to analyze system behavior, including fluid dynamics and thermodynamic considerations, and apply failure analysis techniques for reliable device development.

title: INTRODUCTION TO CLINICAL ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the design, selection, and application of medical technologies within healthcare environments. It delves into the properties of materials used in medical devices, their interaction with biological systems, and the mechanics of biological fluids relevant to device function. Emphasis is placed on understanding device performance, evaluating risks, and ensuring patient safety through rigorous testing and analysis. The academic scope covers essential concepts from biomaterials science, fluid mechanics, thermodynamics, and instrumentation as they apply to clinical practice.

title: THERMODYNAMICS OF BIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of thermodynamics and their application to biological systems. It delves into the laws governing energy, heat, and work, and their implications for spontaneity and equilibrium in biological processes. Students will learn to analyze energy transformations, understand the role of entropy, and apply thermodynamic concepts to molecular interactions and cellular functions. The academic scope covers both theoretical foundations and practical considerations relevant to biomedical engineering.

outline.title: Work and Energy in Biological Systems

title: BIOMATERIALS ENGINEERING I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of biomaterials, focusing on their definition, classification, and essential properties. It delves into the critical aspects of biocompatibility, biodegradability, and the evaluation methods used to assess their suitability for medical applications. The curriculum covers various classes of biomaterials, including polymers, ceramics, and metals, examining their chemical and mechanical characteristics and their diverse applications in healthcare.

title: BIOMECHANICS I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the behavior of materials and fluids within biological systems. It delves into the characteristics, selection, and evaluation of biomaterials used in medical devices and implants. Additionally, it examines the laws of thermodynamics as applied to biological processes and the mechanics of fluid flow within the body, including pressure dynamics. The academic scope covers material science, fluid dynamics, and thermodynamics relevant to biomedical engineering applications.

title: INTRODUCTION TO MOLECULAR AND CELL BIOLOGY
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of the molecular basis of life and cellular processes. It explores the structure, properties, and functions of key biomolecules, including carbohydrates, nucleic acids, and proteins. Students will learn about genetic information flow, biochemical transformations, and the molecular mechanisms underpinning biological systems.

title: MECHANISMS OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the operation of biomedical devices. It delves into the properties and selection of materials used in medical applications, the fluid dynamics and thermodynamics relevant to biological systems, and the electronic components that enable device functionality. The academic scope includes understanding how these elements interact to achieve diagnostic, therapeutic, or monitoring objectives within the human body.

title: DIGITAL CONTROL SYSTEMS FOR BME
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of measurement and instrumentation principles essential for analyzing and controlling biological systems. It explores the dynamics of biological fluids and the theoretical basis for acquiring and processing signals from living organisms. The academic scope encompasses sensor technologies, signal analysis, error management, and the fundamental concepts required for developing advanced biomedical control applications.

title: INTRODUCTION TO BIONIC PROSTHESIS AND ORTHOTICS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles behind the design, selection, and application of biomaterials for bionic prostheses and orthotic devices. It covers the properties of various material classes, including polymers, metals, and ceramics, and their interactions with biological systems. Emphasis is placed on understanding biocompatibility, degradation, mechanical performance, and evaluation methods crucial for developing effective artificial limbs and supportive devices.

title: SYSTEMS BIOENGINEERING II
description: This course examines the fundamental principles and advanced applications in biomedical engineering systems. It covers the behavior of biological materials, fluid dynamics, and thermodynamic processes within living organisms. Emphasis is placed on the design, analysis, and reliability of biomedical devices and systems, incorporating principles of measurement and failure assessment.

title: CLINICAL REFRIDGIRATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
description: This course focuses on the fundamental principles and engineering applications of refrigeration and air conditioning systems within clinical and healthcare environments. It covers the thermodynamic cycles, fluid mechanics, and material science relevant to designing, operating, and maintaining HVAC systems essential for patient safety, medical equipment functionality, and laboratory conditions. Emphasis is placed on ensuring precise environmental control, air quality, and system reliability in critical healthcare settings.

title: BIOMATERIALS ENGINEERING II
description: This course delves into the advanced principles governing the design, evaluation, and application of materials used in biological systems. It explores the complex interactions between engineered materials and living tissues, focusing on mechanisms of biocompatibility, degradation, and host response. Students will gain a comprehensive understanding of material properties, characterization techniques, and processing methods critical for developing next-generation biomedical devices and therapeutic solutions.

title: BIOMECHANICS II
description: This advanced course applies mechanical engineering principles to complex biological systems. It focuses on the quantitative analysis of fluid mechanics within physiological contexts, such as blood flow and tissue perfusion. The curriculum also investigates the interaction of mechanical forces with biological tissues and the critical role of material selection in the design of medical devices and implants.

title: INTRODUCTION TO PHARMACOLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of how chemical agents interact with biological systems, emphasizing the engineering and material science aspects relevant to therapeutic interventions. It examines the biochemical basis of biological targets, the physiological transport phenomena, and the critical role of biomaterials in delivery systems. The academic scope covers understanding molecular mechanisms, biocompatibility considerations, and the physiological context of efficacy and safety.

title: BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN
description: This course delves into the critical aspects of creating medical devices, focusing on the interplay between engineering principles and biological systems. It covers the selection, characterization, and performance evaluation of biomaterials, as well as the application of mechanical and thermodynamic laws relevant to biological environments. Students will gain expertise in analytical techniques for device design, risk assessment, and the integration of measurement systems for ensuring device safety and efficacy.

outline.title: Design of Orthopedic and Implant Devices

title: BIOMEDICAL WORKSHOP AND MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical considerations involved in creating biomedical devices. It covers the selection and properties of biomaterials, essential mechanical design theories, and the critical aspects of quality assurance and risk management in production. Students will learn how material characteristics and design choices influence manufacturing processes and device performance. The academic scope includes understanding material behavior, design validation, and risk mitigation strategies relevant to the biomedical engineering field.

title: ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS FOR BIOMEDICAL APPLICATION
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced applications of artificial neural networks within the biomedical domain. Students will learn how to design, train, and evaluate neural network models for tasks such as medical image analysis, disease diagnosis, drug discovery, and physiological signal processing. The academic scope covers theoretical underpinnings, practical implementation techniques, and ethical considerations in deploying AI in healthcare.

outline.title: ANNs in Genomics and Drug Discovery

title: RELIABILITY AND SAFETY OF BIOMEDICAL EQUIPMENT
description: This course explores the critical aspects of ensuring biomedical equipment functions safely and reliably throughout its lifecycle. It covers fundamental principles of biocompatibility, material selection, and design considerations to prevent failures. Students will learn advanced techniques for failure analysis, risk management, and the importance of accurate measurement and instrumentation for patient safety. The academic scope includes understanding material degradation and physiological parameter monitoring relevant to device performance.

title: EMBEDDED SYSTEMS FOR BIOMEDICAL APPLICATION
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical applications of designing and implementing computational systems for biomedical devices. It covers the integration of hardware components like microcontrollers and sensors with real-time software, focusing on data acquisition, signal processing, and communication protocols. The academic scope includes ensuring system reliability, safety, and performance for diagnostic, monitoring, and therapeutic medical technologies.

title: BIO-INFORMATICS
description: This course delves into the computational methodologies essential for analyzing complex biological data. It covers the principles of bioinformatics, focusing on the interpretation of genomic, proteomic, and molecular data through computational tools and algorithms. The academic scope includes database management, sequence analysis, structural bioinformatics, and the application of programming paradigms to biological problems.

title: ADVANCED BIO-SIGNAL PROCESSING
description: This course delves into advanced techniques for analyzing biological signals. It covers sophisticated methods for signal acquisition, processing, and interpretation, focusing on extracting meaningful information from complex physiological data. Students will explore advanced algorithms and computational approaches essential for understanding biological systems through their electrical and mechanical outputs.

title: BIOMEDICAL RADIATION TECHNOLOGY AND IMAGE PROCESSING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and engineering aspects of radiation-based medical imaging technologies. It covers the physics of radiation interaction with matter, the design and operation of imaging systems like X-ray and CT scanners, and advanced techniques for processing, analyzing, and interpreting medical images. The academic scope includes image acquisition, reconstruction, enhancement, quality assessment, and the management of medical imaging data.

title: HEAT TRANSFER IN LIVING TISSUES
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of thermodynamics and heat transfer as applied to biological systems. It delves into the mechanisms of heat generation, transport, and exchange within living tissues and biomolecules. The academic scope includes analyzing thermal phenomena, understanding energy balance, and investigating thermal transitions relevant to physiological processes and biomedical applications.

outline.title: Heat Transfer Calculations in Biological Systems

title: BIOSAFETY AND HOSPITAL HEALTH ENGINEERING
description: This course integrates engineering principles with safety protocols essential for healthcare environments. It examines the selection, evaluation, and safe application of materials used in medical devices and hospital infrastructure. The curriculum emphasizes risk assessment, infection control strategies, and the engineering of systems critical for patient and staff well-being within healthcare facilities.

title: INTRODUCTION TO CARDIOVASCULAR FLUID MECHANICS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of fluid mechanics as applied to biological systems. It delves into the behavior of fluids, including their motion, pressure dynamics, and rheological properties. Emphasis is placed on understanding fluid flow within biological conduits, such as blood vessels, and analyzing the forces and pressures that govern physiological processes. The academic scope covers theoretical frameworks and their application to biological phenomena.

title: INTRODUCTION TO GENERAL PATHOLOGY
description: This course examines the fundamental principles governing biological systems at molecular, cellular, and macroscopic levels. It explores the chemical composition and interactions of biomolecules, the physical laws dictating fluid behavior within organisms, and the interplay between biological tissues and engineered materials. Understanding these core concepts provides a basis for analyzing physiological functions and deviations from normal states.

title: TISSUE ENGINEERING AND REGENERATIVE MEDICINE
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of biomaterials and their application in creating functional tissues and organs. It delves into material properties, biocompatibility, degradation, and the design of scaffolds that support cellular growth and tissue regeneration. The academic scope covers cell-material interactions, regenerative medicine strategies, and the biochemical and mechanical considerations essential for successful biomedical applications.

title: COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS FOR MEDICAL DEVICES
description: This course explores the principles and applications of computational methods in the design and analysis of medical devices. It covers advanced simulation techniques, material behavior modeling, and performance evaluation under physiological conditions. Students will learn to utilize software tools for creating virtual prototypes, predicting device functionality, and ensuring safety and efficacy in biomedical engineering applications.

title: BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING INDUSTRIES
description: This course examines the industrial aspects of biomedical engineering, focusing on the selection, design, and evaluation of materials and devices for medical applications. It covers essential principles of manufacturing, quality assurance, and risk management crucial for the commercialization of biomedical technologies. The academic scope bridges fundamental scientific knowledge with practical industrial considerations to ensure the development of safe and effective medical products.

title: CLINICAL APPLICATION OF HUMAN COMPUTER INTERACTION
description: This course explores the principles and practices of designing and developing software for clinical applications within biomedical engineering. It covers fundamental object-oriented programming concepts essential for building robust medical systems, focusing on user interface design, data management, and the integration of software into clinical workflows. The academic scope includes understanding how to create, clean, and display patient data effectively, ensuring usability and safety in healthcare technology.

outline.title: User Interface Design for Medical Devices

title: BIOMEDICAL TELEMETRY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced applications of transmitting biological data remotely. It covers the design and implementation of systems for acquiring, processing, and wirelessly communicating physiological signals. The academic scope includes sensor technologies, signal conditioning, data encoding, wireless communication protocols, and the critical aspects of biocompatibility, safety, and reliability for biomedical devices.

outline.title: Reliability and Risk Management for Medical Devices

title: APPLICATION OF MICROFLUIDICS IN MEDICAL TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the principles and applications of microfluidic systems in modern medical technology. It delves into the fundamental fluid dynamics, material science, and biological interactions relevant to designing and implementing microscale devices for diagnostics, therapeutics, and research. Students will gain an understanding of how manipulating fluids at the micro-level enables novel approaches to healthcare challenges.

title: INTRODUCTION TO BIO-TRANSPORT PROCESSES
description: This course introduces the fundamental principles governing the transport of mass, momentum, and energy within biological systems. It explores the physical laws that describe fluid flow, heat transfer, and diffusion in biological contexts. Students will learn to analyze transport phenomena in various physiological systems, applying theoretical concepts to solve practical biomedical engineering problems.

title: SKELETAL TISSUE MECHANICS
description: This course delves into the mechanical behavior and properties of biological tissues that form the musculoskeletal system. It examines the principles governing the response of bone, cartilage, and other skeletal components to mechanical loads. Furthermore, the course investigates the selection, characterization, and application of biomaterials used in orthopedic implants and prosthetics, focusing on their performance under physiological conditions.

title: RESEARCH PROJECT I
description: This course delves into advanced topics within Biomedical Engineering, focusing on the theoretical underpinnings and practical applications essential for research. Students will explore the properties and evaluation of biomaterials, the mechanics and thermodynamics of biological systems, and fundamental biochemical processes. The curriculum also covers essential instrumentation, computational methods, and programming techniques relevant to biomedical research.

title: RESEARCH PROJECT II
description: This course focuses on the application of advanced biomedical engineering principles to conduct independent research. Students will explore the theoretical underpinnings and practical methodologies required for investigating complex biological systems and engineered devices. The scope includes analyzing material properties, fluid dynamics, biochemical processes, and measurement techniques relevant to biomedical innovation and problem-solving.

title: CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LABORATORY
description: This laboratory course provides hands-on experience in fundamental chemical engineering unit operations. Students will investigate heat and mass transfer phenomena in a cooling tower, focusing on experimental design, data acquisition, and performance analysis. Key aspects include understanding psychrometric principles, fluid dynamics, and the impact of operating variables on system efficiency. The course emphasizes practical application of theoretical concepts through rigorous experimentation and data interpretation.

title: INTRODUCTION TO MATERIAL AND ENERGY BALANCES
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of mass and energy conservation principles as applied to chemical engineering processes. Students will learn to formulate and solve material and energy balances for various systems, including those involving chemical reactions and phase changes. Emphasis is placed on developing systematic problem-solving strategies and applying these concepts to analyze and design chemical processes.

title: TRANSPORT PHENOMENA II
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing heat transfer, including its physical origins and rate equations. It delves into the three primary modes: conduction, convection, and radiation, and their mathematical modeling. The academic scope includes analytical and numerical solution techniques for steady-state and transient problems across various geometries.

title: TRANSPORT PHENOMENA I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the movement and behavior of fluids. It delves into concepts such as fluid properties, statics, and dynamics, including flow regimes and governing equations. The curriculum also encompasses the transfer of momentum, heat, and mass, essential for understanding chemical engineering processes. Applications range from flow in conduits to the design of fluid handling equipment.

title: CHEMICAL KINETICS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the rates and mechanisms of chemical reactions. It delves into the analysis of kinetic data, the design and performance of various reactor types, and the influence of thermodynamics and transport phenomena. Emphasis is placed on developing analytical and problem-solving skills for chemical reaction engineering applications.

title: CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of thermodynamics and their application to chemical engineering processes. It covers the scope and origin of thermodynamic laws, the analysis of energy and mass balances for closed and open systems, and the behavior of pure substances and mixtures. Emphasis is placed on understanding thermodynamic properties, phase equilibria, and chemical reaction equilibria, providing a basis for process analysis and design.

title: SEPERATION PROCESSES I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and applications of separation processes crucial in chemical and biological industries. It covers transport phenomena, including mass, heat, and momentum transfer, as a basis for understanding various unit operations. Students will delve into methods such as distillation, absorption, extraction, membrane technologies, and mechanical separations, with an emphasis on process design and analysis.

outline.title: Introduction to Separation Processes and Transport Phenomena

title: BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING I
description: This course explores fundamental biochemical processes and their engineering applications. It covers metabolic pathways like glycolysis, enzyme kinetics and regulation, and microbial growth dynamics. Students will learn to model and analyze these biological systems within an engineering context.

title: SCIENCE OF MATERIALS
description: This course explores the fundamental relationships between the processing, structure, properties, and performance of engineering materials. It delves into the atomic and molecular basis of material behavior, including various types of bonding and crystal structures. Students will examine diverse material classes such as ceramics, composites, metals, polymers, and semiconductors, understanding their unique characteristics and applications.

title: POLYMER PROCESS ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of polymer processing engineering. It covers the molecular structure, polymerization mechanisms, and essential properties of polymeric materials. Students will gain an understanding of various processing techniques, including injection moulding, extrusion, and thermoforming, and how material characteristics influence product formation and application.

title: PROCESS MODELLING AND STIMULATION
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles and practical applications of process modeling and simulation within chemical engineering. It covers the development of mathematical models to represent complex systems and the utilization of simulation software, primarily Aspen HYSYS, for analyzing process behavior and predicting responses. Key areas include constructing process flowsheets, defining material and energy streams, modeling unit operations, and understanding simulation convergence techniques. The academic scope extends to basic process control concepts and dynamic simulation methods.

title: PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION
description: This course introduces the fundamental principles and practical applications of instrumentation and control within chemical engineering processes. It covers the selection, calibration, and application of various measurement devices for key process variables such as flow, temperature, pressure, and level. Students will learn to interpret process diagrams, understand control strategies like PID, and analyze system behavior for effective process operation and troubleshooting.

title: PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and industrial applications of particle technology. It covers the characterization, handling, and processing of particulate solids, which are ubiquitous in various process industries. Topics include particle properties, size reduction, separation techniques, fluidization, filtration, and measurement methods, bridging the gap between fundamental particle behavior and bulk material properties.

title: CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LABORATORY II
description: This course offers practical experience in chemical engineering experimentation, focusing on the application of fundamental principles to real-world processes. Students will engage in hands-on laboratory work, analyze experimental data using computational tools, and explore concepts in process dynamics and control. The curriculum emphasizes understanding transport phenomena, reaction kinetics, and process optimization through experimental investigation and simulation. A key component involves developing skills in experimental design, data interpretation, and technical communication for engineering practice.

title: TRANSPORT PHENOMENA III
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles of transport phenomena, focusing on the interconnectedness of momentum, heat, and mass transfer. It explores advanced topics such as unsteady-state and convective transport, turbulent flow, and boundary layer theory. The academic scope extends to the application of these principles in key chemical engineering unit operations like evaporation and drying, as well as the behavior of non-Newtonian fluids.

title: CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING I
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing chemical transformations within industrial processes. It explores the kinetics of chemical reactions, the design and analysis of various reactor types, and the application of mass and energy balances to reactive systems. The academic scope includes understanding reaction mechanisms, optimizing reactor performance, and analyzing factors that influence reaction rates and product distribution.

title: CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS II
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles of chemical engineering thermodynamics, focusing on the quantitative determination of thermodynamic properties for various substances. It covers ideal gas behavior, real gas correlations, and the thermodynamic properties associated with phase changes in liquids and solids. Students will learn to utilize extensive property tables and generalized charts to analyze and predict thermodynamic states and processes relevant to chemical engineering applications. The academic scope emphasizes the application of thermodynamic laws to understand and quantify energy and property relationships in chemical systems.

outline.title: Ideal Gas Enthalpy and Internal Energy

title: PLANT DESIGN I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practices involved in designing chemical production facilities. It covers the entire project lifecycle, from initial concept and economic evaluation to detailed engineering and startup. Key areas include process flow diagramming, material and energy balances, equipment selection, cost estimation, and site considerations.

title: SEPERATION PROCESS II
description: This course delves into advanced principles and applications of separation processes crucial in chemical engineering. It covers complex separation techniques such as multicomponent distillation, various membrane filtration methods, and adsorption-based separations. Students will explore the design and operation of equipment for crystallization, solid-liquid extraction, and drying, alongside advanced mass transfer phenomena. The academic scope includes theoretical underpinnings, practical design considerations, and the selection of appropriate technologies for industrial challenges.

title: CHEMICAL ENGINEERING ANALYSIS
description: This course provides a comprehensive foundation in the analytical principles and quantitative methods essential for chemical engineering. It covers fundamental concepts in thermodynamics, fluid mechanics, heat and mass transfer, and chemical reaction engineering. Students will learn to apply these principles to analyze and solve complex problems in process design, optimization, and economic evaluation. The curriculum also emphasizes the use of modern computational tools, including spreadsheet software and process simulators, to enhance analytical capabilities and prepare for professional practice.

title: ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the principles and practices of managing environmental impacts within chemical processes. It covers the assessment of pollution, the design and operation of control technologies for air, water, and waste streams, and the integration of sustainability into chemical manufacturing. Emphasis is placed on regulatory compliance, process safety, and the application of fundamental chemical engineering concepts to environmental challenges.

title: RESEARCH PROJECT
description: This course focuses on the application of advanced chemical engineering principles and analytical techniques to investigate complex industrial problems. Students will explore methodologies for process analysis, modeling, and optimization. The academic scope encompasses the development and application of computational tools and mathematical frameworks for understanding and solving engineering challenges, leading to the synthesis and presentation of research findings.

title: CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LABORATORY III
description: This laboratory course provides hands-on experience in applying fundamental chemical engineering principles to real-world processes. Students will conduct experiments to verify theoretical concepts in areas such as fluid mechanics, heat and mass transfer, and reaction engineering. Emphasis is placed on experimental design, data acquisition, rigorous analysis using computational tools, and the interpretation of results to understand process behavior and optimize operations. The course also integrates principles of process safety and loss prevention relevant to industrial practice.

title: PROCESS OPTIMISATION
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced techniques of process optimization, focusing on the mathematical formulation of chemical engineering problems. It covers the development of models, definition of objective functions and constraints, and the application of analytical and numerical methods to find optimal solutions. Emphasis is placed on computational tools and practical applications in process design, operation, and control within the chemical industry.

title: LOSS PREVENTION IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
description: This course focuses on the principles and practices essential for preventing accidents and minimizing losses in chemical process industries. It covers hazard identification, risk assessment methodologies, and the implementation of safety systems to ensure operational integrity. Students will learn to analyze accident statistics, understand safety regulations, and design inherently safer processes to protect personnel, assets, and the environment.

title: CHEMICAL REACTION ENGINEERING II
description: This course delves into the advanced principles of chemical reaction engineering, focusing on the design, analysis, and optimization of reactors for complex reaction systems. It explores the kinetics and mechanisms governing chemical transformations and their translation into practical reactor designs. Furthermore, the curriculum integrates process control strategies essential for managing and stabilizing reactive processes, ensuring efficient and safe operation in industrial settings.

title: PROCESS CONTROL II
description: This course delves into the advanced principles and applications of process control systems within chemical engineering. It covers the dynamic behavior of chemical processes, the design and analysis of control loops, and the implementation of various control strategies. Emphasis is placed on understanding system stability, controller tuning, and the practical aspects of instrumentation and control system design for industrial operations. The academic scope includes theoretical analysis and practical considerations for achieving efficient and safe process operation.

title: PROCESS CONTROL I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical applications of process control systems. It covers the dynamics of chemical processes, the design and implementation of control strategies, and the role of instrumentation and automation in industrial settings. Students will learn to analyze system behavior, develop transfer functions, and understand various control algorithms used to maintain process stability and optimize performance.

title: RESERVOIR ENGINEERING
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing the behavior of hydrocarbon reservoirs. It explores the geological and physical characteristics that define reservoir quality and influence fluid flow. Students will learn about the key elements of a reservoir, the primary activities involved in its management, and the technical responsibilities of engineers in optimizing hydrocarbon recovery. The academic scope covers the science and engineering required to understand, evaluate, and manage underground petroleum accumulations.

title: SEPERATION PROCESSES III
description: This course delves into advanced principles and methodologies for separating chemical components. It covers the theoretical underpinnings and practical design considerations for various separation techniques, including equilibrium-stage operations, continuous contactors, and specialized processes like extraction, adsorption, membrane separation, and crystallization. Emphasis is placed on understanding mass transfer phenomena, process simulation, and factors influencing equipment performance and capacity.

title: PLANT DESIGN II
description: This course delves into the advanced principles and practical considerations of chemical plant design. It covers the synthesis of process ideas, detailed equipment selection, and comprehensive economic evaluation. Students will explore site selection, plant layout, environmental impact mitigation, and project management aspects crucial for successful chemical manufacturing facility development.

title: POLYMER SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of polymer science and technology. It covers the synthesis, structure, and characterization of macromolecules, delving into the relationship between molecular architecture and macroscopic properties. Students will investigate various physical and mechanical behaviors, alongside common processing techniques and applications of polymeric materials.

title: PETROCHEMICAL SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of chemical engineering applied to the petroleum and petrochemical industries. It covers the characterization of crude oil, various refining processes, and the properties of derived products. Emphasis is placed on understanding the thermodynamics, reaction engineering, and separation techniques crucial for processing hydrocarbons, alongside essential aspects of process safety and advanced heavy oil treatments.

title: BIOCHEMICAL ENGINEERING II
description: This course delves into advanced principles governing biological processes within chemical engineering contexts. It explores the design, analysis, and control of systems utilizing biological components like enzymes and microorganisms. Emphasis is placed on understanding metabolic pathways, bioreactor dynamics, and the separation of biomolecules for industrial applications. The academic scope covers theoretical foundations and practical considerations for bioprocess development and optimization.

title: FERMENTATION TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced applications of biological process engineering. It covers the design, operation, and control of bioreactors, focusing on mass and energy transfer, reaction kinetics, and microbial growth dynamics. Students will also delve into the critical aspects of upstream and downstream processing, including separation, purification, and scale-up strategies for biotechnological products. The academic scope encompasses both theoretical underpinnings and practical considerations for industrial fermentation processes.

title: TECHNOLOGY PF FOSSIL FUEL PROCESSING
description: This course delves into the intricate technologies and processes employed in the transformation of fossil fuels into essential products. It examines the conversion of crude oil and natural gas into fuels, chemicals, and materials, covering fundamental refining operations and advanced catalytic conversion techniques. The curriculum also addresses the upgrading of heavy feedstocks and the integration of environmental control strategies within the industry.

title: COAL PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced technologies involved in transforming raw coal into useful products. It covers material and energy balances, reaction kinetics, reactor design, separation processes, and process optimization relevant to coal conversion. Emphasis is placed on understanding the chemical and physical transformations, as well as safety and efficiency considerations in industrial-scale operations.

title: SUGAR TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental chemical engineering principles applied to the processing of sugar. It covers the extraction of sucrose from raw materials, followed by purification, concentration through evaporation, and controlled crystallization to achieve desired product purity and crystal size. Emphasis is placed on separation techniques, heat and mass transfer phenomena, process control, and the valorization of by-products.

title: CEMENT AND CEMENT TECHNOLOGY
description: This course delves into the fundamental chemical and physical processes involved in cement manufacturing. It explores the chemistry of cementitious materials, the thermodynamics and kinetics of high-temperature reactions like calcination and clinkering, and the engineering principles behind process design and operation. Emphasis will be placed on understanding raw material selection, cement hydration, different cement types, and quality control measures essential for the industry.

title: DETERGENT TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental chemical engineering principles applied to the design, operation, and optimization of detergent manufacturing processes. It covers core concepts including mass and energy balances, thermodynamics, fluid mechanics, heat and mass transfer, and chemical reaction engineering. Students will gain an understanding of raw material chemistry, common production techniques, process control, quality assurance, and safety protocols essential for the industry.

outline.title: Safety and Loss Prevention in Production

title: PULP AND PAPER TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the application of chemical engineering principles to the processing of polymers and their integration into paper-based products. It covers fundamental concepts in fluid mechanics, heat and mass transfer, and process design, alongside specialized polymer processing techniques such as coating and extrusion. Emphasis is placed on understanding material properties, process control, and the design of systems for producing advanced paper materials and coatings.

title: PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced technologies involved in transforming crude oil into valuable petroleum products. It covers the entire refining process, from feedstock characterization and separation techniques to complex conversion processes and operational management. Emphasis is placed on understanding process flowsheets, product specifications, and modern advancements in petroleum processing for cleaner fuels and efficient operations.

title: GENERAL CHEMISTRY I
description: This course introduces fundamental principles of chemistry, focusing on the behavior of matter and energy. It covers essential concepts such as atomic structure, chemical bonding, and the properties of solutions. Students will explore the quantitative aspects of chemical reactions, including equilibrium and kinetics, and gain an understanding of basic thermodynamic principles.

title: GENERAL CHEMISTRY II
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of chemical principles, with a significant emphasis on the structure, properties, and reactions of organic compounds. It explores the reactivity of various functional groups, including alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, alcohols, carbonyls, and amines. Additionally, the course covers fundamental concepts in inorganic chemistry, states of matter, and essential laboratory techniques for purification and separation.

outline.title: States of Matter and Solid Structures

title: GENERAL PRACTICAL CHEMISTRY 1
description: This course introduces fundamental chemical principles and their practical applications in laboratory settings. It covers essential concepts such as chemical reactions, equilibrium, acid-base behavior, and kinetics. Emphasis is placed on developing skills in experimental observation, data analysis, and the interpretation of chemical phenomena through quantitative methods.

title: GENERAL PRACTICAL CHEMISTRY II
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of chemical principles and laboratory techniques essential for scientific disciplines. It covers fundamental concepts in organic chemistry, including nomenclature, isomerism, and various reaction types. Emphasis is placed on practical laboratory skills, purification methods, and the identification of organic compounds, preparing students for further scientific study.

title: INTRODUCTION TO COMPUTER ENGINEERING
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of computer engineering principles. It explores the fundamental building blocks of computer systems, including their architecture, hardware components, and the logic that governs their operation. Students will learn about number systems, digital logic gates, and how these elements are combined to create functional circuits. The curriculum also touches upon the interaction between hardware and basic system software, offering a broad perspective on how computing devices are designed and function.

title: OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
description: This course explores fundamental principles of object-oriented design and implementation. Students will learn to model complex systems using classes and objects, understand inheritance and polymorphism, and build reusable software components. The curriculum covers the creation of class hierarchies and the design of interfaces for abstract concepts.

title: COMPUTER ARCHITECTURE AND ORGANIZATION
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of computer system organization and operation. It examines how hardware components are structured and interact to support software execution, focusing on the architecture that enables efficient processing, memory management, and input/output operations. Key areas include the design of processors, memory hierarchies, storage systems, and the mechanisms for managing concurrent processes and system resources.

title: COMPUTER ENGINEERING LABORATORY
description: This laboratory course provides hands-on experience with fundamental computer engineering principles. Students will engage in practical experiments covering digital logic design, basic computer architecture, and operating system concepts. The course emphasizes developing experimental design skills, data analysis, and the ability to implement and test theoretical concepts in a practical setting.

title: COMPUTER ENGINEERING LABORATORY
description: This laboratory course provides hands-on experience with fundamental computer engineering principles. Students will engage in practical experiments covering digital logic design, operating system functionalities, and software development techniques. The course emphasizes experimental design, data analysis, and the application of theoretical concepts to real-world engineering problems.

title: ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS AND DATA STRUCTURES
description: This course explores the design and analysis of efficient computational methods for solving complex problems. It covers fundamental algorithmic paradigms, including various search strategies, optimization techniques, and methods for artificial intelligence applications. Students will learn to evaluate algorithm performance based on complexity and other critical properties, enabling them to select and implement appropriate solutions for diverse computational challenges.

outline.title: Data Structures for AI and Search

title: SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT TECHNIQUES
description: The course focuses on the systematic approach to designing, developing, and maintaining software systems. It explores fundamental principles and practices that ensure software quality, reliability, and efficiency. Students will learn about various development methodologies, design concepts, and the importance of engineering principles in overcoming software development challenges.

title: NUMERICAL COMPUTATIONS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and techniques used in numerical computation. It covers the representation of numbers in computers, the analysis and minimization of errors inherent in numerical methods, and the application of computational algorithms to solve mathematical problems. Emphasis is placed on understanding the mathematical basis of these methods and their practical implementation.

title: PROTOTYPING TECHNIQUES
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical techniques of creating functional models of systems. It covers the entire prototyping lifecycle, from initial concept validation and design simulation to physical construction and testing. Emphasis is placed on electronic prototyping, including the use of simulation tools, PCB design, soldering, and packaging, to transform ideas into tangible representations for evaluation and refinement.

title: COMPUTER ENGINEERING LABORATORY III
description: This laboratory course provides hands-on experience with fundamental computer engineering concepts. Students will engage in practical exercises covering operating system principles, including process, memory, and I/O management. The course also emphasizes hardware prototyping techniques, from initial design and simulation to functional implementation and testing. Students will develop practical skills in building and evaluating computer systems through laboratory experiments.

outline.title: Operating System I/O and File Systems

title: OPERATING SYSTEM
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and architecture of operating systems. It covers essential concepts such as process management, CPU scheduling, memory allocation, and file system organization.
