Students will gain an understanding of system structures, inter-process communication, I/O operations, and mechanisms for handling deadlocks and concurrency. The academic scope includes both theoretical foundations and practical considerations in modern operating system design.

title: ASSEMBLY LANGUAGE PROGRAMMING
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the fundamental principles of low-level computer programming. Students will gain an in-depth understanding of processor architecture, instruction sets, and memory management. The curriculum focuses on writing efficient code directly for the hardware, enabling students to grasp the intricacies of software-hardware interaction. The academic scope covers the translation of abstract concepts into concrete machine instructions and the optimization of programs for performance and resource utilization.

title: DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of transmitting information between devices. It covers the components of a communication system, various transmission media, and techniques for encoding data into signals. Students will learn about network structures, including Local and Wide Area Networks, and the underlying technologies of the Internet. The curriculum also delves into data link control, switching methods, and essential protocols that enable communication across interconnected systems.

title: CONTROL THEORY AND SYSTEMS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the behavior and management of complex systems. It delves into techniques for ensuring reliable operation, managing resource allocation, and maintaining system integrity under various conditions. The academic scope includes understanding system interactions, mitigating disturbances, and implementing robust communication and data handling strategies.

title: ARTIFICIAL NEUTRAL NETWORK
description: This course explores advanced deep learning techniques applied to natural language processing. It covers fundamental neural network architectures, including RNNs, LSTMs, and CNNs, and their application in tasks such as text classification, sequence prediction, and information retrieval. Emphasis is placed on text representation methods, model training, and evaluation using real-world datasets.

title: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the fundamental principles and techniques of artificial intelligence and machine learning. It explores core concepts such as search algorithms, knowledge representation, logical reasoning, and various machine learning paradigms. The curriculum also covers advanced topics including expert systems, probabilistic methods, planning, and applications in areas like machine vision and natural language processing.

title: COMPUTER SECURITY TECNIQUES
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles of computer security, emphasizing confidentiality, integrity, and availability. It explores various attack vectors, malicious programs such as viruses and worms, and their corresponding countermeasures. Students will gain knowledge of intrusion detection, firewalls, access control mechanisms, cryptography, digital signatures, and organizational security practices, including policy, ethics, risk assessment, and testing.

title: EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
description: This course explores the principles and methodologies for designing embedded systems. It covers the architectural components, systematic design processes, and hardware-software integration essential for creating dedicated-purpose computing systems. Emphasis is placed on modular design, requirement definition, implementation strategies, and the crucial stages of testing, debugging, documentation, and maintenance.

title: SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the systematic, disciplined, and quantifiable application of engineering principles to the development, operation, and maintenance of software. It covers the entire software lifecycle, from understanding user requirements and designing robust solutions to implementing, testing, and evolving complex software systems. The academic scope includes principles for creating efficient, reliable, and high-quality software products.

title: DIGITAL SYSTEM DESIGN WITH VDHL
description: This course delves into the principles and methodologies for designing digital systems. It covers the analysis and synthesis of combinational and sequential logic circuits, including state machine design. Students will explore fundamental building blocks such as logic gates, flip-flops, registers, counters, and memory elements. The curriculum also encompasses number systems, Boolean algebra, and interfaces like Analog-to-Digital and Digital-to-Analog converters.

title: MODELLING AND SIMULATION OF COMPUTER SYSTEMS
description: This course delves into the principles and techniques for creating abstract representations of complex systems, particularly focusing on computer systems. It covers the entire simulation modeling lifecycle, from problem definition and data collection to model development, validation, and analysis of experimental results. Students will learn to apply simulation methodologies to understand system behavior, evaluate design alternatives, and optimize performance.

title: COMPUTER GRAPHICS AND ANIMATION
description: Computer graphics encompasses the creation and manipulation of digital images, including animated visuals. It is a dynamic field where images displayed on screens are fundamentally composed of pixels arranged in a grid. Understanding the distinction between raster and vector graphics, along with coordinate systems and transformations, is crucial for representing and rendering these images effectively.

title: MICROPROCESSOR SYSTEM AND INTERFACING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical applications of microprocessor systems. It covers the architecture of microprocessors, their interaction with memory and peripheral devices, and the design of embedded systems. Emphasis is placed on understanding input/output mechanisms, bus structures, and interfacing techniques essential for building functional microprocessor-based applications.

title: DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and techniques for processing signals in the digital domain. It covers the theoretical underpinnings of digital signal processing, including signal representation, transformations, and analysis. The curriculum also delves into the methods for converting between analog and digital signal formats, essential for interfacing real-world phenomena with digital systems. Furthermore, it examines various signal encoding strategies used in data communications and digital systems.

title: RELIABILITY AND MAINTAINABILITY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of reliability and maintainability in engineering systems. It covers methods for analyzing system dependability, predicting failure probabilities, and designing systems to withstand operational stresses. Students will learn techniques for assessing system performance, identifying potential failure modes, and implementing strategies to enhance longevity and reduce downtime.

title: CYBER-PRENEURSHIP AND CYBER LAW
description: This course explores the dynamic intersection of entrepreneurship and the digital realm. It delves into the foundational concepts of establishing and managing online ventures, covering essential entrepreneurial skills, business models, and legal structures pertinent to the internet economy. Furthermore, it examines the legal landscape governing online activities, including intellectual property, data privacy, media law, and the implications of cybercrime for businesses operating in the digital space.

title: NATURAL LANGUAGE AND SPEECH PROCESSING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and techniques for enabling computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language. It covers the linguistic underpinnings of language, including syntax, semantics, and phonology, and delves into computational methods for processing text. Key areas include formal grammars, parsing, ambiguity resolution, and the architecture of natural language processing systems, along with their diverse applications.

title: DIGITAL IMAGING PROCESSING
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles and techniques for processing signals in the digital domain. It explores signal representation, manipulation, analysis, and the underlying systems required for digital signal processing. Emphasis is placed on understanding the advantages and applications of digital methods over analog approaches, including signal transformations and encoding, providing a foundation for advanced signal analysis.

title: FUZZY LOGIC AND PROGRAMMING
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the theory and application of fuzzy logic and artificial neural networks. It covers fundamental concepts such as fuzzy sets, membership functions, fuzzy rules, and inference mechanisms. Students will explore the design and implementation of fuzzy expert systems, fuzzy logic controllers, and neuro-fuzzy hybrid systems. The academic scope emphasizes practical problem-solving using these computational intelligence paradigms.

title: CRYPTOGRAPHY PRINCIPLES AND APPLICATIONS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of computer security, focusing on confidentiality, integrity, and availability. It delves into various cryptographic techniques, including encryption methods, digital signatures, and the analysis of attacks and security services. The curriculum also covers practical aspects such as intrusion detection, access control mechanisms, and the overview of security audits and risk assessment.

title: ROBOTIC AND AUTOMATION
description: This course delves into the core principles and practical applications of robotics and automation. It covers the fundamental concepts of robot kinematics, dynamics, components, and control systems. The curriculum also explores the architecture and development environment of robotic operating systems, alongside robot design considerations and industrial integration strategies. Students will gain a comprehensive understanding of how to design, program, and deploy robotic systems for various applications.

title: INTRODUCTION TO MOBILE COMPUTING
description: This course explores the fundamental concepts, technologies, and applications of mobile computing and wireless communications. It delves into the technical challenges posed by mobile devices and wireless media, examining various standards, protocols, and their strengths and weaknesses. The curriculum provides a critical evaluation of existing and emerging technologies, focusing on network layers 1-3 and relevant transport/application issues, to equip students with the knowledge to design and manage wireless networks.

outline.title: QoS and Security in Mobile Networks

title: DESIGN AND INSTALLATION OF ELECTRICAL DEVICES AND ICT
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical techniques involved in the design, prototyping, and installation of electrical devices and Information and Communication Technology (ICT) systems. It covers digital logic design, circuit fabrication, system simulation, and the integration of hardware components. Emphasis is placed on understanding electronic enclosures, printed circuit boards, and the overall process of bringing a functional prototype to a deployable state, including considerations for usability and packaging.

title: PROJECT
description: This course covers the comprehensive process of engineering project development and documentation. It guides students through the creation of project proposals, from identifying research problems and defining clear objectives to outlining methodologies and planning project activities. Emphasis is placed on conducting literature reviews, collecting and analyzing data, interpreting results, and discussing findings within a scientific context. The course culminates in the structured compilation of a final research report, including all necessary components from introduction to conclusion.

title: BASIC CIVIL ENGINEERING
description: This course introduces the fundamental principles and broad scope of civil engineering. It explores the definition, role, and responsibilities of the profession, along with its various specialized branches and their applications. The curriculum covers essential aspects such as site investigation, construction materials, project management, and surveying techniques. Emphasis is placed on understanding the societal impact and ethical considerations inherent in civil engineering practice.

title: BASIC HYDROLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the Earth's water systems. It covers the continuous movement of water through the hydrologic cycle, including processes like precipitation, infiltration, evaporation, and runoff. The academic scope includes understanding water resources, their measurement, and the application of hydrological knowledge to solve environmental and engineering problems.

title: FLUID MECHANICS FOR CIVIL ENGINEERS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the behavior and motion of fluids. It covers fluid properties, statics, kinematics, and dynamics, including the equations of motion and energy. Applications in civil engineering are emphasized, such as flow in pipes, open channels, hydraulic structures, and the operation of hydraulic machinery like turbines and pumps. The course also touches upon flow measurement techniques and basic fluidic devices.

title: STRUCTURAL MECHANICS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the behavior of structural elements under various loads. It covers the analysis of stress and strain, material properties, and deformation characteristics of components subjected to axial loads, torsion, and bending. Advanced topics include stress transformation, column buckling, and the application of matrix methods for analyzing complex indeterminate structures.

title: ADVANCED STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing the behavior of structural elements under load. It explores the concepts of stress, strain, and material properties, leading to the analysis of bending moments and shear forces within beams. Students will learn to calculate internal forces, draw shear force and bending moment diagrams, and determine stresses and deformations in various beam cross-sections and loading conditions. The academic scope includes understanding beam behavior, calculating critical values, and applying theoretical knowledge to practical engineering problems.

outline.title: Moment of Inertia and Section Modulus
Shear Force and Bending Moment Definitions
Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams

title: SOIL MECHANICS
description: This course explores the fundamental physical properties of soils and their behavior. It covers essential weight-volume relationships, key soil properties like unit weight and void ratio, and introduces various systems for classifying soils based on their characteristics. The academic scope includes understanding soil composition, particle interactions, and the principles behind soil classification for engineering applications.

title: CIVIL ENGINEERING MATERIALS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and properties of various materials critical to civil engineering practice. It covers the characteristics, production, and applications of essential construction materials including cement, concrete, aggregates, bituminous binders, polymers, ceramics, glass, bricks, steel, and rubber. Emphasis is placed on understanding material behavior, selection criteria, and their performance in diverse construction scenarios.

title: STRUCTURAL DESIGN I
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of structural behavior and the principles of structural design. It covers methods for analyzing determinate structures, including the calculation of internal forces and deformations. Students will learn to apply these analytical techniques to the design of common structural elements, considering various loading conditions and design philosophies to ensure safety and economy.

outline.title: Philosophy and Process of Structural Design

title: THEORY OF STRUCTURES
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of structural analysis for civil engineering applications. It covers the behavior of statically determinate structures, including beams, trusses, and arches, under various loading conditions. Students will learn to determine reactions, internal forces, and stress resultants using equilibrium equations and analytical methods. The course also introduces concepts of structural idealization, determinacy, stability, and influence lines, providing a foundation for understanding more complex structural systems.

title: ENGINEERING SURVEYING II
description: This course explores advanced surveying techniques essential for modern engineering projects. It covers the principles and applications of photogrammetry for data acquisition and analysis, alongside methods for precise setting out of construction elements. Students will also learn to calculate earthworks volumes and understand the role of Global Positioning Systems (GPS) in various surveying tasks. The academic scope includes theoretical foundations and practical considerations for these critical surveying disciplines.

title: ENGINEERING SURVEYING
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of engineering surveying principles and practices. It covers essential techniques for establishing horizontal and vertical control networks, crucial for the precise setting out of engineering projects. Students will learn about various surveying methods, equipment, error analysis, and data recording essential for accurate site surveys and construction control.

title: CIVIL ENGINEERING DRAWING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and applications of computer-aided drafting (CAD) within the field of civil engineering. Students will learn to utilize CAD software for creating precise technical drawings, understanding various drawing types, and managing drawing elements effectively. The curriculum covers essential CAD commands, coordinate systems, layer management, and the practical application of CAD in civil engineering design and construction.

outline.title: Applications of CAD in Civil Engineering

title: ENGINEERING GEOLOGY
description: Engineering geology applies geological principles to civil engineering projects, focusing on understanding ground conditions and their influence on design and construction. It covers the study of earth materials, geological structures, surface processes, and groundwater. The course also addresses geological hazards, site investigation techniques, and the application of geological information in planning and development to ensure safe and sustainable infrastructure.

title: CIVIL ENGINEERING PRACTICE
description: This course explores the multifaceted nature of civil engineering practice, covering its broad scope and diverse sub-disciplines. It delves into the critical aspects of project initiation, including contract law, tendering procedures, and public procurement regulations. Students will learn about project planning, control techniques, management principles, and the administration of civil engineering works from commissioning to completion, emphasizing standards and stakeholder roles.

title: HYDRAULICS ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing fluid mechanics, with a focus on water in natural and engineered systems. It covers the analysis of flow in open channels and closed conduits, including classifications, energy concepts, and hydraulic design considerations. Students will gain proficiency in applying theoretical knowledge to solve practical problems in water resources engineering, pipe networks, and hydraulic structures, often utilizing computational tools for analysis.

title: STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and methods for analyzing pin-jointed truss structures. It covers the idealization of trusses, assumptions for analysis, and criteria for determining statical determinacy and stability. Students will learn to calculate internal forces using methods such as joints, sections, and tension coefficients, and will investigate truss behavior under various loading conditions. Advanced topics include the application of virtual work and energy methods for determining displacements and reactions in statically determinate and indeterminate systems.

outline.title: Analysis of Trusses: Method of Joints
Analysis of Trusses: Method of Sections

title: STRUCTURAL DESIGN II
description: This course delves into the principles and practices of structural design for concrete elements. It covers the analysis of various load types, including dead, live, and imposed loads, and their application to structural components. Emphasis is placed on the design of slabs and beams, incorporating reinforcement detailing, deflection control, and shear resistance according to relevant design codes. The academic scope includes understanding structural behavior under load and ensuring safety and serviceability through rigorous calculations.

title: FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the behavior of soils under applied loads. It delves into the calculation of stress distribution within soil masses due to various surface loading conditions, including concentrated, rectangular, and circular loads. Emphasis is placed on understanding soil compressibility, consolidation phenomena, and the prediction of settlements, as well as the determination of ultimate bearing capacity for foundation design.

title: HIGHWAY ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of highway engineering, focusing on the geometric design and planning of road networks. It covers the critical aspects of highway alignment, including horizontal and vertical considerations, and the factors that influence these decisions. Students will also learn about pavement structures, material properties, and the importance of soil investigations and stabilization for durable road construction.

title: STRUCTURAL ENGINEERING
description: This course delves into the principles and applications of structural dynamics, focusing on the behavior of structures under time-varying loads. It covers the formulation of equations of motion, methods of discretization, and advanced analytical techniques such as finite element analysis and modal analysis. The curriculum also explores specialized topics including seismic response, vibration control, beams on elastic foundations, offshore structures, and stochastic vibration analysis, providing a comprehensive understanding of dynamic structural behavior.

title: WATER RESOURCES ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical applications of water resources engineering. It covers the development, control, and management of water resources, addressing challenges such as floods, droughts, and water quality. Students will learn about the design and analysis of hydraulic structures, groundwater management, water supply systems, and sustainable resource utilization.

title: HIGHWAY & TRANSPORTATION ENGINEERING I
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the principles and practices of highway and transportation engineering. It covers the planning, design, and construction of transportation facilities, including road development, geometric design, pavement engineering, and traffic management. The curriculum also delves into essential aspects such as transportation economics, drainage systems, and bridge engineering, aiming to equip students with the knowledge to create safe, efficient, and sustainable transportation infrastructure.

title: STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS II
description: This course delves into advanced methods for analyzing structural behavior under various loading conditions. It covers the application of matrix methods, including stiffness matrix derivations for different structural elements, and displacement-based techniques like the slope-deflection method. Students will explore the analysis of complex systems such as trusses, beams, and frames, as well as understand material behavior beyond the elastic limit, including elastoplasticity and yield line theory. The course emphasizes structural idealization and the calculation of displacements, forces, and stresses in indeterminate structures.

title: ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING
description: This course applies science and engineering principles to protect the environment and human health. It covers the design and management of systems for waste treatment, pollution control, and resource protection. Students will explore concepts related to environmental impact assessment, sustainable development, and the roles of engineers in addressing environmental challenges.

title: BUILDING SERVICES ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and applications of building services engineering. It covers the design, installation, and management of essential building systems including water supply, drainage, HVAC, electrical, lighting, and fire safety. Emphasis is placed on understanding material properties, transportation systems, waste management, and environmental considerations within the built environment. The academic scope includes technical knowledge, system integration, and performance evaluation for efficient and sustainable building operations.

title: INTRODUCTION TO ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
description: This course introduces students to the fundamental principles of electrical and electronics engineering. It covers essential concepts such as electric charge, current, voltage, resistance, and power. Students will explore basic circuit analysis techniques, including Ohm's Law and Kirchhoff's Laws, and gain an understanding of ideal and practical sources. The curriculum also touches upon the basics of electrical measurement and instrumentation.

title: ELECTRONICS I
description: Electronics is the branch of engineering concerned with the behavior and control of electric currents through vacuum, gas, or semiconductor devices. This course explores fundamental electronic devices and their utilization in circuits. It covers the distinction between analog and digital signals and systems, laying the groundwork for understanding modern electronic design.

title: ELECTRONICS II (ANALOGUE)
description: This course provides a comprehensive study of analog electronic circuits, focusing on their analysis and design. It explores various amplifier types, including multistage and power amplifiers, alongside the fundamental principles and applications of operational amplifiers. The curriculum also covers signal generation techniques, switching circuits, and the calculation of power and efficiency in electronic systems.

title: ELECTROMAGNETIC FIELD AND WAVES I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of electrostatics, focusing on the behavior of static electric charges and the fields they generate. It covers Coulomb's Law for calculating forces between charges, the concept of electric field intensity, and methods for determining electric fields from various charge distributions, including continuous ones. Students will also learn about electric potential, the work done in electric fields, Gauss's Law for simplifying field calculations, and the properties of electric flux density and dipoles.

title: ELECTROMAGNETIC FIELD AND WAVE THEORY II
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing electromagnetic fields and waves, extending beyond static phenomena. It explores the dynamic behavior of electric and magnetic fields, culminating in the formulation of Maxwell's equations. The curriculum covers the propagation characteristics of electromagnetic waves in various media, including free space and conducting materials, and examines wave polarization. Advanced topics include transmission line theory, antenna principles, and electromagnetic radiation, providing a comprehensive understanding of wave phenomena and their applications in modern technologies.

title: ELECTRICAL/ELECTRONICS LABORATORY I
description: This laboratory course provides hands-on experience with fundamental electrical and electronic principles. Students will conduct experiments to verify theoretical concepts, develop practical circuit analysis skills, and gain proficiency in using essential laboratory equipment. The scope covers DC and AC circuit behavior, semiconductor device characteristics, and basic amplifier configurations.

outline.title: First and Second Order Circuit Transients

title: ELECTRICAL/ELECTRONIC LABORATORY
description: This course provides hands-on experience with fundamental electrical and electronic principles through laboratory experiments. Students will verify theoretical concepts, develop practical skills in measurement and analysis, and gain proficiency in operating laboratory equipment. The scope covers circuit analysis, machine characteristics, instrumentation techniques, control system elements, and signal processing applications.

title: MEASUREMENT AND INSTRUMENTATION
description: This course provides a comprehensive study of the principles and applications of measurement and instrumentation systems. It covers fundamental concepts of units, standards, and measurement system design. Students will explore various types of instruments, their operating principles, characteristics, and limitations. Emphasis is placed on understanding measurement errors, calibration techniques, and the selection of appropriate instruments for diverse engineering applications.

title: ELECTRIC CIRCUIT THEORY I
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of electrical circuits, covering fundamental laws and analysis techniques. Students will learn to analyze resistive, capacitive, and inductive circuits, including transient and steady-state behavior. The curriculum emphasizes applying circuit laws and methods to solve complex networks, preparing students for advanced electrical engineering studies.

title: ELECTRIC CIRCUIT THEORY II
description: This course explores advanced analytical techniques for electrical circuits, focusing on transient behavior and frequency domain analysis. Students will master the application of Laplace transforms for solving complex circuit dynamics and understand resonance phenomena. The curriculum also includes network theorems, non-sinusoidal waveform analysis, and the behavior of non-linear circuit elements.

title: LINEAR SYSTEMS
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles of linear control systems, focusing on their mathematical modeling and analytical techniques. Students will explore methods for representing physical systems, analyzing their behavior in both time and frequency domains, and assessing stability. The curriculum also covers state-space representation, essential design techniques, and an introduction to digital and optimal control concepts.

title: ELECTRICAL MACHINES I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of electromechanical energy conversion, focusing on the construction, operation, and characteristics of direct current (DC) machines. Students will delve into the theories behind generators and motors, including essential components, winding techniques, armature reactions, and commutation. The course also covers the materials used in machine construction, various types of losses, and introduces concepts related to AC machines and actuators.

outline.title: DC Machine Excitation and Speed Control
Materials and Losses in Electrical Machines

title: ELECTRICAL MACHINES II
description: This course explores the principles and applications of electrical machines, focusing on energy conversion and machine design. It covers the construction, theory of operation, and equivalent circuit modeling for synchronous machines, including generators and motors. Key topics include performance analysis, power factor control, parallel operation, and an introduction to AC and DC machine construction, alongside magnetic circuit analogies.

outline.title: DC Machine Construction and Compound Types

title: COMMUNICATION PRINCIPLES
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and diverse applications of electronic communication systems. It covers essential concepts such as modulation, multiplexing, signal transmission, and reception across various media. Students will gain an understanding of analog and digital communication techniques, radio frequency fundamentals, and modern wireless technologies. The curriculum also delves into specialized areas like satellite, optical, and microwave communications, preparing students for careers in the evolving field.

title: SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT TECHNIQUES
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practices of software development. It covers the entire software development life cycle, from initial problem analysis and algorithm design to various design methodologies and implementation techniques. Students will learn about structured programming constructs, modular design, and different software development paradigms, with an introduction to the C programming language and its application. The academic scope includes understanding software processes, designing efficient solutions, and basic testing and maintenance.

title: NUMERICAL COMPUTATIONS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and techniques of numerical computation, essential for solving complex mathematical problems using computers. It covers the representation of numbers, the analysis of approximation and various types of errors inherent in computational processes. Students will learn methods for numerical differentiation, solving nonlinear and linear systems, interpolation, and approximating solutions to ordinary differential equations.

title: ASSEMBLY LANGUAGE PROGRAMMING
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles of assembly language programming, focusing on its direct interaction with computer hardware. Students will explore data representation, processor architecture, and the instruction set, learning to write low-level code for specific tasks. The curriculum covers essential programming constructs like loops, procedures, and memory management, alongside the use of development tools such as assemblers and debuggers. Emphasis is placed on understanding the relationship between assembly language and machine code, enabling students to develop efficient and hardware-aware applications.

title: ELECTRICAL/ELECTRONICS LABORATORY III
description: This laboratory course provides hands-on experience in fundamental electrical and electronic measurement techniques. Students will explore the principles of instrumentation, control systems, and signal processing through practical experiments. The course emphasizes the application of theoretical concepts to real-world systems, fostering skills in experimental design, data analysis, and system troubleshooting.

title: DATA COMMUNICATION AND NETWORKS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of exchanging data between devices. It covers the architecture, components, and operational models of computer networks. Students will gain knowledge of various protocols, transmission methods, error handling, and network layers essential for modern communication systems.

outline.title: Introduction to Data Communication and Networks

title: ELECTRIC POWER PRINCIPLES
description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of electric power systems, detailing their fundamental structure from generation to distribution. It explores various conventional and non-conventional power generation methods and their operational principles. A significant focus is placed on the economic operation of power systems, including economic dispatch and the minimization of fuel costs. The course also covers essential concepts related to power factor and its correction for efficient system performance.

title: CONTROL THEORY AND SERVOMECHANISM
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of control theory and its application in servomechanism systems. It covers the mathematical modeling of dynamic systems, analysis of system behavior using transfer functions and state-space representations, and the design of controllers to achieve desired performance. Emphasis is placed on understanding feedback mechanisms, system stability, and the components that constitute modern control systems.

title: DIGITAL ELECTRONICS AND SYSTEMS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of digital electronics, focusing on the representation and manipulation of data in digital form. It covers essential concepts such as number systems, Boolean algebra, and logic gates, leading to the design of combinational and sequential circuits. Applications in computer systems, communication, and control systems are examined, along with the use of key components like flip-flops, registers, and memory devices.

title: ELECTRICAL SERVICES DESIGN
description: This course explores the design of robust electrical systems, focusing on principles of reliability, maintainability, and availability. It covers the analysis of system and component failures, and methods for enhancing operational integrity. The curriculum also delves into control system architectures, including analog and digital controllers, and the application of automation technologies like Programmable Logic Controllers. Furthermore, students will examine advanced design techniques, the use of computational intelligence, and the integration of various system components to ensure optimal performance and adherence to engineering standards.

title: EMBEDDED SYSTEM
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical applications of computing systems integrated into larger devices and systems. It covers the design, development, and deployment of specialized hardware and software components, focusing on real-time operation and efficient resource utilization. The academic scope includes understanding how these systems interact with their environment to perform specific functions.

title: DIGITAL SYSTEM DESIGN WITH VDHL PROGRAMMING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of digital electronics and logic design. It covers the design and analysis of combinational and sequential circuits, including essential building blocks like logic gates, flip-flops, registers, and counters. The curriculum also delves into memory systems, basic CPU architecture, and the application of digital systems in areas such as control and signal generation. Emphasis is placed on understanding digital system components and their integration into functional systems.

title: MODELLING AND SIMULATION OF ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS SYSTEMS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical applications of modeling and simulation for electrical and electronics systems. It covers the methodologies for developing simulation models, designing experiments, and analyzing results, alongside the essential computer-aided design (CAD) techniques used in modern engineering. Students will learn to utilize EDA tools for schematic capture, PCB layout, and component design, understanding how these processes integrate with simulation for system verification and optimization. The academic scope encompasses both theoretical underpinnings and hands-on design practices critical for engineering professionals.

title: ADVANCED ELECTRONICS
description: This course explores the principles and applications of linear integrated circuits, which process analog signals to produce outputs linearly related to inputs. It covers fundamental components like operational amplifiers, detailing their linear and non-linear operations, and various configurations. The curriculum also delves into essential circuits such as voltage regulators, comparators, and analog-to-digital/digital-to-analog converters, highlighting their design considerations and practical uses in modern electronic systems.

title: DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the fundamental principles and techniques of digital signal processing. It covers the representation and analysis of signals and systems in both time and transform domains, including discrete-time Fourier transforms, z-transforms, and discrete Fourier transforms. Emphasis is placed on understanding sampling theory, digital filter design, and the practical implementation of DSP algorithms, along with exploring various real-world applications.

outline.title: The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) and FFT

title: DIGITAL COMMUNICATION
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and building blocks of transmitting information in digital form. It covers the architecture of digital communication systems, including source and channel encoding, digital modulation techniques, and the characteristics of communication channels. Emphasis is placed on system performance, error control, and the advantages offered by digital transmission methods.

title: RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced techniques for ensuring the dependability of engineering systems. It covers probabilistic methods, failure analysis, and mathematical models used to quantify reliability. Students will learn to evaluate system reliability through various configurations and prediction methods, and understand the practical aspects of implementing reliability programs in design and operation. The academic scope includes both theoretical foundations and applications in diverse engineering fields.

title: INDUSTRIAL ELECTRONICS DESIGNS
description: This course delves into the design and implementation of electronic systems for industrial applications. It covers advanced control strategies, automation techniques, and the integration of microcontrollers and digital systems. Students will explore principles of reliability engineering, industrial communication protocols, and power electronics essential for modern manufacturing and process control environments. The academic scope includes theoretical foundations and practical considerations for building robust and efficient industrial electronic solutions.

title: HIGH FREQUENCY AND MICROWAVE ELECTRONICS
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles and design techniques for electronic circuits operating at high frequencies and microwave spectrums. It covers the behavior of electromagnetic waves, transmission line theory, and impedance matching for efficient power transfer. Students will explore the analysis and design of active and passive components, including amplifiers, oscillators, filters, and waveguides, crucial for modern communication and radar systems.

title: POWER SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
description: This course provides a comprehensive study of power system analysis and operation. It covers the formulation and iterative solution of load flow equations, methods for load forecasting, and the principles of power generation and voltage control. Students will also explore power system stability, protection systems, fault analysis, and communication techniques, equipping them with essential skills for power system engineering.

title: POWER SYSTEMS COMMUNICATION AND CONTROL
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced applications of control systems, communication techniques, and power system engineering. It covers the analysis and design of feedback control loops, digital and analog control strategies, and various communication methods relevant to power infrastructure. Emphasis is placed on understanding power system dynamics, protection, and the integration of control and communication technologies for reliable operation.

outline.title: Power System Load Flow and Stability

title: HIGH VOLTAGE ENGINEERING
description: This course provides a comprehensive understanding of high voltage power systems, covering their historical development, network components, and fundamental equipment. It delves into the behavior of electrical fields, the properties and applications of various insulating materials, and the generation of high voltages for testing. Emphasis is placed on analyzing power system overvoltages, implementing insulation coordination strategies, and understanding surge protection devices. The curriculum also addresses critical high voltage safety principles and laboratory practices.

outline.title: Introduction to High Voltage Power Systems

title: ELECTRIC DRIVES AND APPLICATIONS
description: This course delves into the principles and applications of electric drives, focusing on the synergy between electric machines, power electronic converters, and sophisticated control systems. It explores the design, analysis, and implementation of drive systems for various applications, including electric propulsion and industrial automation. Key aspects such as energy efficiency, system reliability, and the role of microcontrollers in modern drive control are critically examined.

title: ELECTRICAL ENERGY CONVERSION AND STORAGE
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing the conversion of electrical energy to mechanical energy and vice versa, as well as the storage of electrical energy. It explores the role of magnetic fields in these processes, covering energy balance, force and torque development, and the performance metrics of energy conversion devices. The academic scope includes theoretical analysis of electromechanical systems, their efficiency, regulation, and various applications.

title: CONTROL ENGINEERING
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles of control engineering, focusing on the analysis and design of dynamic systems. It covers state-space representations, transfer functions, and system stability using eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Key concepts of controllability and observability are explored, leading to advanced topics such as state feedback control, modal control, observers, and optimal control strategies like the Linear Quadratic Regulator. The curriculum also introduces nonlinear system modeling and analysis techniques, along with practical applications.

outline.title: State Space Representation of Linear Systems

title: POWER ELECTRONICS
description: This field involves the design and application of electronic circuits and semiconductor devices for the efficient conversion and control of electrical energy. It encompasses techniques for transforming electrical power from one form to another, enabling a wide range of modern technologies. The scope includes understanding device characteristics, circuit topologies, and control methods for high-power applications.

title: SEMINARS ON USE OF ENGINEERING SOFTWARE PACKAGES IN ELECTRICAL/ELECTRONICS FIELD
description: This course explores the application of specialized software packages and programming languages crucial for modern electrical and electronics engineering. It covers the use of computational tools for analysis, design, simulation, and problem-solving across various domains including digital signal processing, control systems, communication networks, and reliability engineering. Students will gain practical experience with software environments that facilitate complex calculations, system modeling, and the implementation of advanced engineering techniques. The curriculum emphasizes developing proficiency in leveraging these tools to enhance engineering workflows and innovation.

title: MODERN CONTROL ENGINEERING
description: This course delves into advanced techniques for analyzing and designing complex control systems. It covers state-space representations, system properties like controllability and observability, and various methods for stability analysis in both time and frequency domains. Emphasis is placed on modern controller design strategies, including state feedback and observer-based control, often utilizing computational tools for system simulation and design.

title: CONTROL ENGINEERING III (NON-LINEAR AND PROCESS AUTOMATION)
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of nonlinear control systems, covering their unique characteristics and methods for classification. It delves into various techniques for modeling and analyzing system behavior, including linearization, the describing function method, phase-plane analysis, and Lyapunov stability theory. The curriculum also introduces the analysis of sampled-data systems, providing a comprehensive foundation for understanding and controlling complex nonlinear processes.

title: MICROCOMPUTER TECHNOLOGY
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles and practical applications of microprocessors and microcontrollers. It covers their internal architecture, instruction set execution, and memory organization. Students will learn to program these devices using both assembly and C languages, and understand how to interface them with various peripherals for embedded system development. The academic scope includes designing, implementing, and debugging embedded systems for diverse applications.

title: BROADCASTING AND INTERNET TECHNOLOGY
description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of modern communication systems, integrating principles of digital communication with the architecture and technologies of the internet. It explores data transmission, network layers, protocols, and switching mechanisms essential for building robust communication infrastructures. The academic scope covers signal processing, error control, media access, and network performance, bridging foundational electronic communication concepts with contemporary internet technologies.

title: STUDENT PROJECT
description: This course focuses on the comprehensive process of undertaking advanced engineering projects. Students will learn to formulate research questions, conduct thorough literature reviews, design and execute experiments or simulations, and analyze resulting data. Emphasis is placed on developing strong technical writing and presentation skills to effectively communicate project findings and contributions within the electrical and electronics engineering domain. The academic scope covers research methodology, technical application, and professional reporting standards.

title: INTRODUCTION TO SCIENCE OF MATERIALS
description: This course introduces the fundamental principles governing the structure, properties, processing, and performance of engineering materials. It explores the interrelationships between atomic structure, microstructure, and macroscopic behavior across various material classes. The academic scope covers the foundational concepts necessary for understanding how materials function in engineering applications and how their properties can be tailored.

title: STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing the mechanical behavior of engineering materials under load. It covers the analysis of stress, strain, and material properties such as elasticity and rigidity. Students will learn to analyze structural components, including beams and shafts, by calculating shear forces, bending moments, and torsional effects. The curriculum also emphasizes determining geometric properties of cross-sections and understanding the practical applications of these concepts in engineering design and failure analysis.

title: INTRODUCTION TO MECHATRONICS ENGINEERING
description: This course explores fundamental principles of mechatronic systems, focusing on the integration of mechanical and electrical components for automated control. It delves into the selection and application of critical components like pneumatic valves, examining their internal designs, operational characteristics, and performance trade-offs. Understanding these elements is crucial for designing efficient and reliable mechatronic solutions.

title: FLUID MECHANICS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the behavior of fluids, both at rest and in motion. It delves into the physical properties of fluids, the forces acting upon them, and the mathematical descriptions of their motion. Key areas include fluid statics, kinematics, dynamics, and the analysis of fluid flow phenomena. The course also examines practical applications across various engineering disciplines.

title: BASIC ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING I
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of electrical engineering principles. It covers fundamental concepts of electric circuits, including DC and AC analysis, and introduces basic electromagnetic phenomena. The academic scope includes problem-solving techniques for circuit analysis and an introduction to electrical components and their behavior. Students will develop skills in applying theoretical knowledge to practical engineering scenarios.

title: BASIC ELECTRICAL ENGINEERING II
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing semiconductor devices. It explores the intrinsic and extrinsic properties of semiconductor materials, including carrier concentration, mobility, and conductivity. A significant focus is placed on understanding the behavior of PN junctions under various biasing conditions, examining concepts like depletion regions, electric fields, and current flow mechanisms. The academic scope covers the physics behind these phenomena, laying the groundwork for analyzing more complex semiconductor circuits and devices.

title: COMPUTER APPLICATIONS
description: This course equips engineering students with essential computational skills for problem-solving. It covers the principles of algorithmic thinking, flowcharting, and basic programming logic to tackle engineering challenges. Students will learn to utilize software tools, particularly spreadsheets, for data processing, analysis, and visualization, enhancing their ability to manage and interpret technical information effectively.

title: COMPUTER PROGRAMMING AND APPLICATIONS
description: This course introduces fundamental principles of computer programming and their application in solving practical problems. Students will learn to design, implement, and debug programs using a procedural programming language. Key concepts include data types, control structures, arithmetic and logical operations, input/output handling, and the use of standard libraries. The course emphasizes algorithmic thinking and the translation of mathematical and logical concepts into executable code.

title: ENGINEERING DRAWING I
description: This course introduces fundamental principles and techniques for creating and interpreting technical drawings. It covers various projection methods, including orthographic, isometric, and oblique, to represent three-dimensional objects in two dimensions. Students will learn to depict internal features using sectional views and understand standard practices for dimensioning and sketching.

title: ENGINEERING DRAWING II
description: This course provides foundational knowledge in engineering sketching and drawing, emphasizing precision and clear communication of technical ideas. Students will learn to create and interpret various pictorial and orthographic representations of objects. Key skills include converting between different projection types such as isometric, oblique, and orthographic views, as well as understanding sectional views and dimensioning principles. The academic scope covers the practical application of graphical tools to convey design intent and technical specifications accurately.

title: ENGINEERING MECHANICS I
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of mechanical principles essential for engineering disciplines. It delves into the analysis of static systems, including forces, moments, and equilibrium conditions, and introduces the study of motion and the forces that cause it in dynamic systems. The academic scope emphasizes problem-solving techniques and the application of mathematical tools to analyze mechanical behavior.

title: ENGINEERING MECHANICS II
description: This course provides a comprehensive study of the principles governing the behavior of mechanical systems and components. It focuses on analyzing forces, motion, and equilibrium in static and dynamic situations. Students will learn to apply mathematical and graphical methods to solve complex engineering problems related to rigid bodies. The curriculum emphasizes understanding the interaction of forces and motion, leading to the design and analysis of mechanical structures and machines.

title: ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS I
description: This course introduces the fundamental principles governing energy transformations and their application in engineering systems. It explores the concepts of heat, work, and internal energy, along with the laws that dictate their behavior. Students will analyze thermodynamic processes and cycles, understand the implications of the second law of thermodynamics, and learn to evaluate the performance of various energy conversion devices.

title: WORKSHOP TECHNOLOGY I
description: This course introduces students to the fundamental principles and practices of engineering workshop technology. It covers essential safety protocols, the identification and use of common hand and machine tools, and basic machining operations. Students will gain practical knowledge in areas such as benchwork, turning, and milling, emphasizing safe and efficient workshop conduct to produce engineering components.

title: WORKSHOP TECHNOLOGY II
description: This course explores fundamental principles of material removal processes used in manufacturing. It covers conventional machining techniques such as turning, drilling, and milling, along with their associated tools and operations. The curriculum also delves into fitting work, hand operations, and the critical parameters that govern machining efficiency and precision.

title: ENGINEERING MATHEMATICS I
description: This course provides a sound foundation in advanced mathematical techniques essential for engineering studies. It emphasizes problem-solving through the application of calculus, linear algebra, and other mathematical tools. The scope covers analytical methods and their practical implementation in various engineering disciplines, building upon foundational mathematical concepts.

title: ENGINEERING MATHEMATICS II
description: This course extends fundamental mathematical techniques essential for engineering disciplines. It covers advanced topics in calculus, including partial differentiation and methods for solving differential equations, both ordinary and partial. The curriculum also delves into optimization techniques, numerical methods for solving equations and systems, and introduces concepts in linear algebra and complex variables, emphasizing practical application and problem-solving using computational tools.

title: ENGINEER IN SOCIETY
description: This course explores the multifaceted relationship between engineering practice and the broader societal context. It examines the evolution of technology, the engineer's role and responsibilities within communities, and the influence of societal factors on engineering development. Emphasis is placed on understanding the interconnectedness of science, technology, and society, and the ethical considerations inherent in engineering practice.

outline.title: Understanding Society and the Engineer's Role

title: STUDENTS' WORK EXPERIENCE PROGRAMME (SWEP I)
description: This course provides essential exposure to the practical realities of the engineering profession through structured work experience. It aims to bridge the gap between academic learning and industry demands, fostering professional growth and understanding of engineering practices. Students will explore the objectives, benefits, and challenges associated with industrial training components within engineering education, preparing them for future careers.

title: COMPUTER PROGRAMMING AND APPLICATIONS
description: This course introduces fundamental programming concepts and their application in C++ for engineering and scientific contexts. It covers problem-solving methodologies, data representation, control structures, and modular programming. The curriculum progresses to object-oriented programming principles, data structures, and numerical techniques relevant to technical fields.

title: ENGINEERING MATHEMATICS III
description: This course delves into advanced mathematical techniques essential for engineering problem-solving. It covers the theory and application of ordinary and higher-order differential equations, including methods for finding general and particular solutions. Students will explore the power of Laplace transforms for solving these equations, alongside fundamental concepts in complex analysis, matrix algebra, and multiple integration. The curriculum also introduces optimization techniques through linear programming and numerical methods for approximation.

outline.title: Matrix Algebra and Systems of Equations

title: ENGINEERING MATHEMATICS IV
description: This course covers essential numerical methods for solving complex engineering problems. It explores techniques for finding roots of equations, interpolating data, and solving systems of linear equations. Students will learn to approximate solutions for ordinary and partial differential equations, and apply statistical methods like regression analysis. The curriculum also emphasizes the practical application of these methods using computational tools such as Excel and MATLAB.

title: ENGINEERING ECONOMICS
description: This discipline applies economic principles to engineering problems, focusing on the systematic evaluation of costs and benefits for technical and business ventures. It equips students with tools to compare alternative projects, analyze cash flows, and understand the time value of money for informed decision-making. Key areas include investment analysis, risk assessment, cost optimization, and the economic justification of engineering solutions to achieve project success and competitive advantage.

title: TECHNICAL WRITING AND PRESENTATION
description: This course explores the principles and practices of technical communication in engineering. It covers the research process, the structure and types of technical documents, and effective methods for presenting scientific and technological information. Emphasis is placed on clarity, objectivity, and audience awareness in written and oral communication.

title: ENGINEERING MANAGEMENT AND LAW
description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of engineering management principles and the legal frameworks essential for engineering practice. It covers organizational structures, management by objectives, and effective project management strategies. The curriculum also delves into critical legal areas such as contract law, intellectual property, company law, and regulatory compliance, alongside financial and economic evaluation techniques relevant to engineering enterprises.

title: ENGINEER IN SOCIETY
description: This course explores the multifaceted role of engineers within society, emphasizing professional conduct and effective collaboration. It delves into essential communication skills, teamwork dynamics, and leadership principles crucial for engineering practice. Furthermore, the curriculum covers fundamental Information and Communication Technologies (ICT), internet applications, and digital tools, examining their application and broader societal impact.

title: ENGINEERING GRAPHICS AND SOLID MODELLING
description: This course introduces fundamental principles of technical drawing and visualization essential for engineering design. Students will learn to create and interpret 2D technical drawings, understand spatial relationships, and gain foundational knowledge in 3D solid modeling techniques. The curriculum emphasizes precision, clarity, and the application of these graphical languages in communicating engineering concepts, particularly within the aerospace domain. It also touches upon effective study strategies and teamwork in design contexts.

title: COMMUNICATION IN ENGLISH I
description: This course aims to equip students with essential communication skills for academic and professional success. It covers foundational principles of effective written and oral expression, including academic writing, presentation techniques, and interpersonal interactions. Students will also develop critical study habits, time management strategies, and the ability to effectively gather and utilize information.

title: NIGERIAN PEOPLE AND CULTURE
description: This course explores the diverse cultural heritage and societal structures of Nigeria. It delves into the historical evolution of Nigerian peoples, examining their traditions, social organizations, and belief systems. Students will gain an understanding of the rich tapestry of ethnic groups, languages, and customs that constitute the Nigerian identity. The academic scope encompasses anthropological, sociological, and historical perspectives on Nigerian society.

title: PEACE STUDIES AND CONFLICT RESOLUTION
description: This course explores the fundamental concepts of conflict, its origins, and various manifestations. It delves into strategies for managing and resolving disputes, examining different conflict handling styles and the stages involved in peacebuilding. Additionally, the curriculum incorporates principles of entrepreneurship, focusing on developing business plans and conducting market research for start-ups, recognizing the role of economic activity in societal stability.

title: PHILOSOPHY AND SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
description: This course explores the intersection of philosophical inquiry and the global pursuit of sustainable development. It delves into the fundamental concepts of philosophy, its various applications, and its relationship with other academic fields. Simultaneously, it examines the framework, history, and specific objectives of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), with a particular focus on their environmental dimensions and target timelines. The academic scope encompasses understanding the philosophical underpinnings of development challenges and how critical thinking can inform solutions for a sustainable future.

title: ENTREPRENEURSHIP AND INNOVATION
description: This course explores the fundamental concepts of entrepreneurship and enterprise, examining various theories that explain entrepreneurial emergence and behavior. It delves into the characteristics and attributes of successful entrepreneurs, their functions within business, and the critical role they play in economic development. Students will learn about the process of identifying business opportunities, generating ideas, and planning for new ventures, including the importance of risk management and innovation. The course also covers different forms of business organization and the practical aspects of developing a comprehensive business plan.

outline.title: Business Idea Generation and Opportunity Recognition

title: PRACTICAL ENTREPRENEURSHIP SKILLS
description: This course explores the fundamental concepts of entrepreneurship, examining its role in economic development and the process of creating new ventures. It delves into the characteristics and theories that define entrepreneurial behavior, alongside practical aspects such as idea generation, business planning, and feasibility studies. Students will gain insights into managing business operations, marketing, and financial considerations essential for establishing and sustaining enterprises.

title: MECHANICAL ACTUATION DESIGN
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and methodologies involved in designing mechanical components and systems. It covers the selection of appropriate materials, analysis of stresses and strains, and consideration of manufacturing processes to ensure functional integrity and economic viability. Students will learn to apply theoretical knowledge to practical design problems, including the design of joints, shafts, gears, and structural elements.

title: SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
description: This course explores the fundamental concepts of signals and the systems that process them. It delves into the mathematical representation, classification, and manipulation of various signal types, including continuous-time and discrete-time signals. Students will learn to analyze system properties such as linearity, time-invariance, and causality, and understand different system models including difference equations and convolution. The academic scope covers time-domain analysis techniques essential for understanding signal processing and system behavior.

title: FLUID MECHANICS II
description: This course delves into the advanced principles governing fluid behavior, extending foundational concepts to complex scenarios. It explores the dynamics of fluids in motion and at rest, focusing on the application of conservation laws to analyze forces, momentum, and energy transfer. Emphasis is placed on developing analytical tools for predicting fluid behavior in engineering systems, including dimensional analysis and the use of measurement devices.

title: MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of modern manufacturing, focusing on the interplay between materials, processes, and production systems. It delves into the characteristics of various engineering materials and the physical and mechanical properties that dictate their use. Students will learn about different manufacturing processes, including material removal, forming, and assembly, as well as the organization and operation of production systems, automation technologies, and considerations for designing products for efficient manufacturing.

title: COMPUTER SOFTWARE ENGINEERING I
description: This course explores fundamental principles of software engineering, focusing on object-oriented programming paradigms and their application in modern software development. Students will learn to design, implement, and manage complex software systems using Python, emphasizing modularity, code organization, and efficient data handling. The curriculum covers core programming concepts, software design patterns, and practical project development, preparing students for building robust and maintainable applications.

outline.title: Introduction to Software Modeling and Simulation

title: ELECTROMECHANICAL DEVICES
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles and advanced analysis techniques for electromechanical systems. It covers the conversion of electrical to mechanical energy, the dynamics and modeling of various electromechanical devices, and the integration of components like actuators, sensors, and power electronics. Emphasis is placed on understanding system behavior, control strategies, and the design process from requirements definition to fabrication.

title: ELECTRONICS I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of electronic circuits, focusing on semiconductor devices and their applications. Students will learn about transistor characteristics, biasing techniques, and amplifier configurations. The curriculum also covers the design and operation of various oscillators, essential for signal generation, and introduces basic concepts in wireless communication systems, including modulation and detection.

title: COMPUTER HARDWARE ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of computer hardware engineering, focusing on the design, architecture, and programming of microprocessors and microcontrollers. Students will investigate instruction set architectures, interrupt handling mechanisms, and the intricacies of bus systems. The curriculum also covers advanced topics such as hardware description languages, programmable logic devices, and the design of central processing units, culminating in an understanding of field-programmable gate arrays.

title: ELECTROMAGNETIC THEORY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of electromagnetic theory, covering static and dynamic electric and magnetic fields. It delves into the behavior of conductors and dielectrics, the concepts of capacitance and inductance, and the formulation of Maxwell's equations. The curriculum extends to wave propagation, transmission lines, and electromagnetic radiation, providing a comprehensive understanding of electromagnetic phenomena.

title: ELECTRIC CIRCUIT THEORY
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles and advanced techniques for analyzing electrical circuits. It covers methods for solving complex networks, including nodal and mesh analysis, and explores various network theorems that simplify circuit calculations. The curriculum also encompasses the design and classification of filters, the synthesis of electrical networks, and the analysis of non-linear circuit behavior and transfer functions.

title: FLUIDICS AND VIRTUAL LABORATORY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of fluid power, encompassing both hydraulic and pneumatic systems. It delves into the design, components, and operational characteristics of these systems, including fluid mechanics concepts and basic circuit analysis. Students will gain an understanding of fluid kinematics, hydraulic laws, and common applications, preparing them for practical implementation and analysis in virtual laboratory environments.

title: CIRCUIT AND SYSTEMS
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of electric circuits, covering fundamental concepts, laws, and analysis techniques essential for electrical engineering. It explores the behavior of circuits with various passive and active elements, including resistors, capacitors, and inductors. The academic scope extends to analyzing circuit responses in both time and frequency domains, and introduces methods for network synthesis and advanced problem-solving.

title: MECHANICS OF MACHINES I
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing the motion of machine components. It explores the study of kinematics, focusing on displacement, velocity, and acceleration without considering the forces involved. Students will analyze various mechanisms, including linkages, pairs, and chains, and learn methods for determining their mobility and motion characteristics. The curriculum covers essential mechanisms like slider-cranks and four-bar systems, along with their inversions and practical applications.

title: ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS II
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing energy conversion and its application in engineering systems. It explores the behavior of thermodynamic systems, the laws that dictate energy transformations, and the properties of substances undergoing various processes. Emphasis is placed on analyzing thermodynamic cycles, understanding energy transfer mechanisms, and evaluating the efficiency and limitations of thermal processes. The academic scope includes advanced topics such as availability, irreversibility, combustion, and compressible flow.

title: CONTROL ENGINEERING
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the fundamental principles of control engineering. It covers the mathematical modeling of dynamic systems, analysis of feedback control system characteristics, and essential techniques for evaluating system performance and stability. Students will explore methods for control system design, including root locus and frequency response techniques, and understand the differences between open-loop and closed-loop configurations.

title: COMPUTER AIDED MANUFACTURING
description: This course explores the integration of computer technology and automation within modern manufacturing environments. It covers the principles of computer-aided design and manufacturing, numerical control systems, and the programming of automated machinery. Students will learn about programmable logic controllers, flexible manufacturing systems, and the strategic implementation of automation to enhance production efficiency and product quality.

title: MEASUREMENT AND INSTRUMENTATION
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical aspects of measurement and instrumentation. It covers the definition, classification, and characteristics of various measuring instruments, alongside the essential elements of measurement systems. The curriculum also delves into the critical concepts of calibration, traceability, and error analysis, preparing students to understand and apply these principles in diverse engineering contexts.

title: DIGITAL SYSTEMS AND PLCS
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the principles and applications of Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) in industrial automation. It covers fundamental PLC hardware and software architectures, including scanning processes and memory organization. Students will explore various PLC programming languages, such as ladder logic, instruction lists, and function blocks, along with essential control logic elements like timers and counters. The curriculum also delves into input/output systems, state-based design, and practical aspects of software development, debugging, and system selection for automated manufacturing environments.

title: ELECTRONICS II
description: This course delves into the analysis and design of digital control systems, exploring discrete-time system dynamics and the application of advanced mathematical tools like the z-transform. It covers fundamental concepts of programmable logic controllers and digital logic design, essential for modern automation. Furthermore, the curriculum examines electromechanical system principles and techniques for modeling and simulating complex systems, preparing students for advanced engineering applications.

title: INTRODUCTION TO ROBOTICS
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of robotic systems, covering their fundamental principles, mathematical modeling, and operational mechanics. It delves into the kinematics and dynamics of manipulators, essential for understanding robot motion and behavior. The curriculum also explores sensor integration, control strategies, and the diverse applications of robots across various industries and environments.

title: VIBRATIONS
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing oscillatory and dynamic systems. It covers mathematical modeling techniques, analysis of system responses, and methods for controlling vibrations. Topics include the behavior of mechanical and electrical oscillators, stability analysis, and frequency domain representations of system dynamics.
