title: CAD/CAM/CNC LABORATORY
description: This laboratory course explores the integration of design, manufacturing, and automation technologies. Students will gain practical experience in creating digital models, generating manufacturing instructions, and understanding the principles of computer-controlled machining. The course emphasizes the translation of engineering designs into tangible products through modern manufacturing processes.

title: PROJECT
description: This course focuses on the application of advanced engineering principles to solve complex industrial and mechatronic problems through a comprehensive project. Students will engage in research, design, simulation, implementation, and evaluation of engineering systems. The academic scope covers system modeling, control design, automation, and integration of mechanical, electrical, and software components, culminating in a detailed technical report and presentation.

title: AUTOMATION MANUFACTURING
description: This course explores the principles and applications of automation in modern manufacturing environments. It covers fundamental concepts of control systems, programmable logic controllers (PLCs), and industrial robotics. Students will delve into computer numerical control (CNC) machining, flexible manufacturing systems (FMS), and process automation strategies. The curriculum also addresses mechatronic systems, actuation technologies, and the economic considerations of implementing automated solutions.

title: DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical applications of processing signals in the digital domain. It covers the conversion of analog signals to digital, the analysis of discrete-time signals and systems, and the design and implementation of digital filters. Students will learn about spectral analysis, adaptive filtering techniques, and specialized areas like multirate processing, with an emphasis on real-world applications in audio, speech, and image processing.

title: MICRO CONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
description: This course explores the foundational principles of digital control systems, including discrete-time analysis, z-transforms, and stability criteria. It covers the architecture, design, and operation of microprocessors and programmable logic controllers, which are central to modern automation. Students will also learn about hardware description languages and their application in developing embedded control solutions for industrial processes.

title: MICROCOMPUTERS AND MICROPROCESSOR SYSTEMS
description: This course explores the fundamental architecture and organization of computer systems, with a focus on microprocessors. It covers the design principles of processor components, memory hierarchies, and I/O systems, alongside the use of hardware description languages for digital circuit design. Students will learn about sequential and concurrent processing, synthesis techniques, and the construction of dedicated and general-purpose microprocessors. The academic scope includes understanding the relationship between hardware implementation and system-level functionality.

outline.title: Introduction to VHDL for Hardware Design

title: COMPUTER SOFTWARES ENGINEERING II
description: This course delves into advanced principles of software development for complex engineering systems. It explores methodologies for designing, implementing, and managing software in domains such as automation, robotics, and control systems. Emphasis is placed on robust software architecture, real-time considerations, and system integration within mechatronic environments.

title: GROUP PROJECT (ROBOT DESIGN AND MANUFACTURE)
description: This course provides hands-on experience in the design, assembly, and programming of robotic systems. Students will explore fundamental principles of robot mechanics, control, and sensor integration through practical projects. The academic scope encompasses understanding robot components, developing functional robotic prototypes, and applying theoretical knowledge to real-world robotic challenges.

outline.title: Lego EV3 Robot Assembly and Hardware

title: SYSTEM MODELLINGS AND SIMULATION
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and methodologies for creating mathematical representations of dynamic systems. It covers techniques for analyzing system behavior, predicting responses, and assessing stability using analytical and computational tools. The curriculum delves into both continuous-time and discrete-time systems, with applications in control engineering and simulation across various domains.

title: CONTROL ENGINEERING II
description: This course delves into advanced methods for analyzing and designing control systems. It covers mathematical modeling using state-space representations, stability analysis through root locus and frequency response techniques, and various controller design strategies. Emphasis is placed on practical design approaches, including pole placement, observer-based control, and introductory concepts of robust control, often utilizing computational tools for simulation and analysis.

title: POWER ELECTRONIC DEVICES
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles and practical applications of semiconductor devices used in power electronics. It covers the characteristics, operation, and control of various power electronic components such as diodes, transistors, and thyristors. The curriculum also explores the design and analysis of power converter topologies (DC-DC, DC-AC, AC-AC) and their integration into electromechanical systems, emphasizing thermal management and system modeling for performance optimization.

title: PROCESS AUTOMATION
description: This course explores the principles and applications of automated control systems. It covers the fundamentals of Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), including ladder logic programming and the implementation of various logic functions. Students will learn to design control sequences for industrial processes, incorporating elements like timers and conditional logic, and gain practical experience through simulation tools.

title: PARTIAL AND FULL AUTOMATION
description: This course delves into the principles and applications of automation across various industrial sectors. It covers the fundamental concepts, components, and strategic considerations for implementing both partial and full automation systems. Key areas include automated manufacturing systems, programmable logic controllers, flexible manufacturing systems, and computer numerical control, alongside the economic and migration aspects of automation adoption.

title: MEMS & VLSI
description: This course explores the design, modeling, and simulation of advanced micro and nano-scale electromechanical systems. It covers hardware description languages like VHDL for digital circuit design and delves into the principles of microcomputer systems and digital signal processing relevant to integrated devices. Students will learn to model system dynamics and simulate performance, bridging the gap between physical phenomena and electronic implementation.

outline.title: Control System Design for Integrated Systems

title: NC AND CNC PRODUCTION MACHINES
description: This course explores the principles and applications of automated manufacturing systems, with a strong emphasis on numerical and computer numerical control (NC and CNC) machine tools. It covers the fundamental concepts of machine control, programming languages such as G-code, and the operational differences between NC and operator-controlled systems. The curriculum also examines the role of Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) and broader automation strategies in enhancing manufacturing efficiency and performance.

title: DESIGN OF SECURITY SYSTEMS
description: This course explores the foundational principles and practical design considerations for building secure systems. It covers the theoretical underpinnings of protection models and delves into the implementation details of access control mechanisms. Students will gain an understanding of policy enforcement, authentication, and the critical attributes that define a secure system, preparing them to design robust security solutions.

title: MICRO FABRICATION TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores advanced manufacturing paradigms, focusing on the integration of automation, control systems, and intelligent production processes. It delves into the principles of automated production, numerical control, flexible manufacturing systems, and programmable logic controllers. Students will gain an understanding of how these technologies enable precision, efficiency, and adaptability in modern industrial environments, particularly in the context of high-precision production.

title: RENEWABLE ENERGY RESOURCES
description: This course delves into the engineering principles and technological advancements driving sustainable energy generation. It explores the conversion of natural resources into usable power, emphasizing system design, performance analysis, and efficient integration. The curriculum covers the planning, control, and economic considerations essential for developing and implementing renewable energy solutions to meet global sustainability goals.

outline.title: Modeling and Simulation of Energy Systems

title: MOBILE ROBOTICS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced concepts of robotic systems, with a particular focus on mobile platforms. It covers the mathematical modeling of robot kinematics and dynamics, essential for understanding robot motion and behavior. Students will investigate sensor integration, perception, navigation strategies, and control systems necessary for autonomous operation, alongside principles of automation in manufacturing and system simulation.

title: LEAN PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT AND INDUSTRIAL LOGISTICS
description: This course examines the integration of lean production principles with industrial logistics to optimize manufacturing operations. It explores the design, implementation, and management of automated systems, including programmable controllers and advanced manufacturing technologies. Key areas of study involve production planning, inventory control, supply chain integration, and the economic impact of automation on industrial processes, aiming to enhance efficiency and productivity.

title: CONTROL ENGINEERING III
description: This course delves into the principles and methodologies of digital control systems, building upon foundational control theory. It covers the transformation of continuous-time systems into discrete-time equivalents, utilizing tools like the z-transform for analysis and design. Key topics include sampling, data reconstruction, system modeling, stability analysis, and performance evaluation in the time and frequency domains. Advanced techniques such as digital controller design, pole assignment, state estimation, and optimal control are also explored.

title: HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the principles of heat and mass transfer. It covers the fundamental modes of energy transport: conduction, convection, and radiation. Students will learn to analyze various heat transfer phenomena, including steady-state and transient conduction, convective heat transfer in different flow regimes, and thermal radiation exchange. The course also introduces heat exchangers and basic mass transfer concepts, equipping students with analytical tools for thermal system design and analysis.

title: INTRODUCTION TO MECHANICAL ENGINEERING
description: This course provides a foundational overview of the field of mechanical engineering, exploring its diverse sub-disciplines and their interrelationships. Students will be introduced to the fundamental principles governing energy, materials, fluids, and mechanical systems. The scope of mechanical engineering's impact on technology and society will be examined, alongside an introduction to the engineering design process and professional practices.

title: MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY
description: This course delves into the core principles and practices of modern manufacturing processes. It examines various production techniques, including casting, welding, and machining, alongside their theoretical underpinnings and practical applications. Students will gain an understanding of manufacturing capabilities, facility layout strategies, and the critical influence of material selection on product design and performance. The curriculum also explores advanced manufacturing methods and process optimization for industrial efficiency.

title: MECHANICS OF MACHINES I
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of the principles governing the motion and forces within mechanical systems. It covers the kinematics and dynamics of particles and rigid bodies, including concepts of displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, work, energy, and momentum. The curriculum also introduces the analysis of mechanisms, vibrations, and friction, essential for comprehending the behavior and design of machines.

title: MECHANICS OF MACHINES II
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the dynamics and behavior of mechanical systems. It focuses on analyzing forces, motion, and energy transfer within machines, including the study of vibrations, balancing of rotating and reciprocating components, and speed regulation mechanisms. The curriculum also addresses the mechanics of gears, friction, and the dynamics of complex linkages, integrating theoretical understanding with practical considerations for machine design and operational efficiency.

title: STRENGTH OF MATERIALS II
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of how materials respond to mechanical forces. It focuses on analyzing stresses and strains within components under various loading conditions, including static and potentially dynamic scenarios. The academic scope involves understanding material behavior, failure modes, and applying these principles to ensure the structural integrity and safe design of mechanical systems.

title: INTRODUCTION TO AUTOCAD
description: This course provides a foundational understanding of essential engineering practices and academic principles. It covers key aspects of technical documentation, effective communication strategies, and the framework of outcome-based education. The curriculum is designed to enhance students' ability to articulate technical information clearly and to understand the objectives and structure of engineering programs.

title: FLUID MECHANICS I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the behavior of fluids at rest and in motion. It covers fluid properties, kinematics, and the application of conservation laws for mass, momentum, and energy. Topics include flow regimes, pipe flow characteristics, head losses, and the distinction between compressible and incompressible flows. The academic scope encompasses theoretical analysis and practical problem-solving related to fluid dynamics phenomena.

title: FLUID MECHANICS II
description: This course delves into advanced principles of fluid dynamics, building upon foundational concepts. It explores the behavior of fluids in motion, focusing on energy losses, flow characteristics in pipes, and the application of dimensional analysis. The academic scope includes understanding complex flow phenomena and their practical implications in engineering systems.

title: MACHINE AND ENGINEERING DESIGN PROCESS
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles of manufacturing processes and engineering design. It explores various methods for shaping and joining materials, including casting, welding, and machining techniques. Emphasis is placed on understanding material properties, selection criteria, and the impact of manufacturing choices on product design and performance. The course also introduces concepts of machine control systems and production facility layouts.

title: ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS II
description: This course delves into advanced principles of thermodynamics, focusing on the analysis and performance of various power generation cycles. It explores the theoretical underpinnings and practical applications of internal combustion engines and gas turbine systems. Emphasis is placed on understanding thermodynamic properties, energy conversion efficiencies, and the factors influencing cycle performance in real-world engineering systems.

title: METROLOGY AND INSTRUMENTATION
description: This course provides a comprehensive understanding of metrology, the science of measurement, and its critical role in engineering. It explores the fundamental principles, objectives, and standards governing precise measurements. Students will gain insights into the various characteristics and quality aspects of measuring instruments, alongside a detailed study of measurement errors, their classification, sources, and analysis. Practical applications, such as angular measurement using specialized tools, will also be covered.

title: COMPUTER IPROGRAMMING AND SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT
description: This course explores the principles and practices of software development, focusing on the systematic creation of robust and efficient software solutions. It delves into understanding program objectives, designing effective learning experiences, and aligning technical outputs with stakeholder expectations. The academic scope encompasses foundational concepts in technical communication and presentation, essential for engineers to convey complex information and project outcomes clearly.

title: COMPUTER APPLICATIONS (CAD/CAM) IN DESIGN AND MANUFACTURE
description: This course explores the application of computer technologies in modern engineering design and manufacturing. It covers the principles of Computer-Aided Design (CAD) for creating and manipulating geometric models, and Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM) for generating machine tool instructions. Students will learn how these tools integrate with various manufacturing processes, CNC machinery, and material selection to optimize product development and production efficiency.

title: ADVANCED MECHANICS OF MATERIALS
description: This course delves into the advanced principles governing the behavior of materials under various mechanical loads. It explores complex stress and strain states, material response under cyclic and sustained loads, and the analysis of structural components subjected to combined loading conditions. Emphasis is placed on understanding failure mechanisms, material selection criteria for demanding applications, and the design of components to ensure safety and reliability in engineering systems.

outline.title: Torsion of Shafts and Power Transmission

title: MECHANICAL VIBRATIONS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of oscillatory motion in mechanical systems. It covers the modeling and analysis of single and multi-degree of freedom systems, including the derivation of equations of motion. Key concepts such as natural frequencies, mode shapes, damping phenomena, and resonance are investigated. The course also examines the practical implications of vibrations, including their detrimental effects and methods for mitigation in engineering design.

title: PRODUCTION ENGINEERING I
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced techniques in modern manufacturing engineering. It covers a wide range of production processes, including casting, welding, machining, and specialized operations like gear manufacturing. The curriculum also emphasizes the strategic aspects of production, such as facility layout, system management, material selection, and industrial applications to enhance operational efficiency and product quality.

title: MACHINE DESIGN II
description: This course explores the advanced principles governing the design of mechanical systems and components. It emphasizes the critical interplay between material properties, manufacturing processes, and product performance. Students will learn to select appropriate materials, analyze stresses and failure mechanisms, and design key machine elements for reliability and efficiency, considering the impact of production methods on design outcomes.

title: METALLURGY AND MATERIALS ENGINEERING
description: This course provides a comprehensive understanding of the principles governing the behavior and properties of engineering materials, with a strong emphasis on metallic systems. It explores the fundamental concepts of metallurgy, including phase transformations, heat treatments, and the processing of ferrous and non-ferrous alloys. The academic scope encompasses material selection, the impact of processing on material performance, and the science behind joining and shaping techniques.

title: INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing internal combustion engines. It explores various engine cycles, including four-stroke and two-stroke operations, and analyzes their thermodynamic processes using P-V diagrams. The curriculum covers combustion phenomena in both petrol and diesel engines, fuel injection systems, and engine timing mechanisms. Additionally, it examines engine performance, efficiency, and methods for enhancing power output such as supercharging.

title: CONTROL SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and applications of control systems in engineering. It covers the analysis and design of systems that regulate and manage processes, distinguishing between open-loop and closed-loop architectures. Key topics include mathematical modeling, feedback mechanisms, controller design, system performance evaluation, and modern control concepts. Students will learn to analyze system behavior, understand component roles, and assess performance metrics for effective engineering solutions.

title: PROJECT
description: This course focuses on the essential skills required for engineering project completion, emphasizing effective technical reporting and presentation. Students will learn to define project objectives, conduct thorough literature reviews, and structure comprehensive technical reports. The curriculum covers the creation of abstracts, executive summaries, and the design and delivery of impactful technical presentations, preparing graduates to communicate complex engineering work clearly and professionally to various stakeholders.

title: MATERIALS HANDLING EQUIPMENT
description: This course delves into the strategic design and operational management of material flow within manufacturing systems. It examines various facility layout strategies and their impact on material movement efficiency. Key manufacturing processes are analyzed to understand their integration into production lines and the associated material handling requirements.

title: MANUFACTURING AUTOMATION
description: This course delves into the principles and applications of automating manufacturing processes. It covers the design and control of automated systems, including advanced machining, robotics, and flexible manufacturing. Students will explore how to integrate control systems, optimize material processing, and manage production operations for enhanced efficiency and quality in modern industrial environments.

title: THEORY OF ELASTICITY
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of how solid materials deform and withstand mechanical loads within their elastic limits. It covers the mathematical formulation of stress and strain, constitutive relationships between them, and the analysis of deformation in various structural components. The academic scope includes understanding material behavior under different loading conditions and applying theoretical solutions to engineering problems.

outline.title: Plane Stress and Plane Strain Analysis

title: TRIBOLOGY
description: This course explores the science and engineering of interacting surfaces in relative motion, focusing on friction, wear, and lubrication. It examines the underlying physical and chemical mechanisms that govern these phenomena, including surface properties, material behavior, and the role of lubricants. The curriculum also emphasizes the development of robust measurement techniques, the analysis of dynamic system responses, and the critical skills required for effective technical communication and reporting of engineering research.

title: SYNTHESIS OF MECHANISM
description: This course explores the design and creation of mechanical systems capable of performing specific functions. It delves into the theoretical principles and practical methodologies for generating novel mechanisms and linkages, covering their kinematic analysis and synthesis. The academic scope extends to understanding how these mechanisms are manufactured, controlled, and integrated into larger engineering systems, ensuring optimal performance and efficiency.

title: INTRODUCTION TO BIOENGINEERING (BIOMATERIALS)
description: This course provides a foundation in the principles and practices essential for advanced bioengineering studies. It emphasizes the critical analysis of scientific literature, the structured development of research proposals, and the effective communication of technical findings. Students will gain proficiency in understanding research methodologies and presenting complex technical information clearly and concisely.

title: PRODUCT DESIGN
description: This course explores the fundamental principles of designing and developing manufactured products. It delves into various manufacturing processes, including casting, machining, and joining techniques, emphasizing their impact on product design. Students will learn to analyze production facility layouts and understand manufacturing capabilities. Furthermore, the course covers essential technical communication skills, focusing on report writing, documentation, and effective presentation of design concepts and results.

title: TURBOMACHINERY
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing the design and operation of turbomachinery. It explores the intricate interplay between fluid dynamics and mechanical energy transfer in devices such as pumps, turbines, and compressors. Students will analyze the performance characteristics, efficiency, and applications of these machines across various engineering domains, including power generation and fluid transport systems.

title: HYDRAULIC AND PNEUMATIC ENGINEERING
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing hydraulic and pneumatic systems. It covers the design, analysis, and operation of fluid power circuits, including component selection, flow characteristics, and energy losses. The academic scope extends to control strategies and performance optimization for efficient power transmission in engineering applications.

title: PROJECT AND MAINTENANCE MANAGEMENT
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and practical applications of managing engineering projects and their subsequent lifecycle. It emphasizes the critical skills required for effective project planning, execution, documentation, and communication. Students will gain proficiency in technical writing, presentation, and reporting, essential for successful project delivery and ensuring the long-term viability of engineered systems.

title: HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing the transfer of thermal energy and mass within various systems. It explores the mechanisms of conduction, convection, and radiation, alongside diffusion processes. Emphasis is placed on applying these concepts to analyze and design thermal systems, including those encountered in materials processing and energy conversion.

title: PIPELINE ENGHINEERING
description: This advanced course delves into the engineering principles and practices essential for the design, analysis, and operation of pipeline systems. It covers fluid mechanics, material selection, structural integrity, and operational control strategies critical for safe and efficient fluid transportation. The curriculum aims to equip students with the knowledge to tackle complex challenges in the pipeline industry.

title: FRACTURE OF STRUCTURAL MATERIALS
description: This course delves into the fundamental mechanisms by which structural materials fail under various loading conditions. It explores the interplay between material properties, applied stresses, and environmental factors that initiate and propagate cracks. Students will learn to analyze failure modes, predict material performance, and understand design considerations to enhance structural integrity and prevent catastrophic events.

title: PRODUCTION ENGINEERING II
description: This course explores advanced manufacturing principles and technologies crucial for modern engineering. It covers the theoretical underpinnings of metal cutting, sophisticated machining techniques like CNC and gear manufacturing, and detailed analysis of casting and welding processes. The curriculum also addresses the strategic aspects of facility layout, material selection, and overall manufacturing capability to optimize production efficiency and product performance.

title: REFRIDGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles of thermodynamics and fluid mechanics as applied to refrigeration and air conditioning systems. It covers the analysis of thermodynamic cycles, the behavior of refrigerants, heat transfer phenomena in system components, and the fluid dynamics governing system operation. The academic scope encompasses the design, analysis, and performance evaluation of cooling and air treatment technologies, emphasizing energy efficiency and system reliability.

title: METROLOGY AND QUALITY CONTROL
description: This course integrates the science of precise measurement with the principles of automated control to ensure product and process quality in mechanical and mechatronics engineering. It covers the fundamental concepts of metrology, characteristics of measuring instruments, and advanced measurement techniques. The academic scope extends to control system theory, including system modeling, feedback mechanisms, and controller design, applied to achieve desired quality standards and process regulation.

title: MACHINE DESIGN III
description: This advanced mechanical engineering course integrates principles of control systems, manufacturing processes, and precision measurement into the design of complex machinery. Students will explore the theoretical underpinnings and practical applications of designing systems with sophisticated control mechanisms, considering manufacturability, fabrication techniques, and metrological requirements. The academic scope encompasses the synthesis of mechanical components with electronic control, advanced fabrication methods, and the establishment of precise design specifications for high-performance machines.

title: TECHNOLOGY POLICY AND PLANNING
description: This course examines the strategic planning and policy frameworks governing technological development and implementation. It focuses on essential communication skills, including technical report writing, effective presentation delivery, and understanding audience needs. The academic scope also includes the design of learning outcomes and stakeholder engagement within engineering and technology programs.

outline.title: Types and Formats of Technical Reports

title: ENERGY TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores advanced principles in energy systems, focusing on the control and dynamics of energy conversion processes. It examines the theoretical frameworks for managing energy flows, optimizing system performance, and ensuring operational stability. Key areas include the application of control theory to energy generation and the analysis of fluid and thermal phenomena critical to energy technology.

title: AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING
description: This course delves into the comprehensive design, analysis, and manufacturing principles of modern automobiles. It covers the intricate systems that constitute a vehicle, including powertrain, chassis, braking, steering, and electrical components. Emphasis is placed on understanding vehicle dynamics, control strategies, safety features, and the application of advanced materials and manufacturing techniques to optimize performance, efficiency, and reliability.

title: ELEMENTARY MATHEMATICS I
description: This course introduces fundamental mathematical reasoning and quantitative analysis. It covers the principles of probability, enabling the understanding of chance and uncertainty, including specific event types. The curriculum also delves into combinatorial methods for counting and arrangement, essential for problem-solving. Additionally, foundational concepts of logic, reasoning, and fallacy identification are explored to develop analytical skills.

title: PETROLEUM PRODUCTION ENGINEERING III
description: This course delves into the advanced principles and practices of petroleum production engineering. It covers the design, implementation, and optimization of well completions, focusing on maximizing hydrocarbon recovery and ensuring operational integrity. Key areas include wellbore performance analysis, stimulation techniques, workover operations, and the selection of appropriate artificial lift and sand control methods to enhance production efficiency throughout the life of a well.

title: MULTIPLE PHASE FLOW IN PIPES
description: This course explores the fundamental principles governing the flow of multiple fluid phases within pipelines. It delves into the identification of various flow regimes, the calculation of pressure drops, and the application of empirical correlations for predicting pipeline hydraulics. Emphasis is placed on understanding phenomena such as liquid dropout in gas lines and transient flow behavior, alongside wellbore performance and non-Darcy flow effects.

title: OIL POLLUTION AND CONTROL
description: This course delves into the fundamental principles governing the behavior and management of hydrocarbon reservoirs and production systems. It covers the analysis of fluid properties, reservoir characteristics, and flow dynamics essential for optimizing hydrocarbon recovery. Emphasis is placed on understanding reservoir performance, wellbore mechanics, and advanced recovery techniques to ensure efficient and controlled extraction of oil and gas resources.

title: OFFSHORE OPERATIONS
description: This course explores the fundamental principles and advanced practices involved in petroleum operations conducted in offshore environments. It covers the design, installation, and operation of offshore production facilities, including fixed platforms, floating systems, and subsea infrastructure. Emphasis is placed on reservoir management, well performance optimization, and the application of specialized technologies for hydrocarbon recovery in challenging marine settings.

title: PETROLEUM REFINING TECHNOLOGY
description: This course explores the fundamental mathematical principles governing fluid flow within porous reservoir media. It covers the derivation and solutions of the diffusivity equation, essential for understanding reservoir behavior. Practical applications include various well testing techniques, their interpretation, and the estimation of key reservoir parameters. Advanced topics address specialized testing methods and the use of software for reservoir characterization and area estimation.
